disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:223453-223491 (DAT_8006f418 = 0x418..0x42f = 12 s16; DAT_8006f430 starts 0x430 with its OWN XREF as a byte table); RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80012d60.c:78 (&DAT_8006f430)[param_3] proves 0x430 is a distinct byte array
fix: Shrink to `static const int16_t DMG_TABLE[12] = {10,20,1000,1000,1000,50,100,200,300,1000,0,0};`. Remove the bogus 771/2569/3595/4111 (not in ROM). Entries 10,11 are 0; no valid index >=12 exists.

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:223453-223491 (DAT_8006f418 = 0x418..0x42f = 12 s16 entries with its own XREF, accessed as *(short*); DAT_8006f430 at 0x430 starts a SEPARATE labeled array with its OWN XREF FUN_80012d60:80012fe8); RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80012d60.c:78 reads (&DAT_8006f430)[param_3] as *(undefined1*) byte = distinct byte array, while :38/:80 read DAT_8006f418 as *(short*). Our code: psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/player_damage_common.c:14-16 declares int16_t[16] {…,771,2569,3595,4111}; :106 indexes DMG_TABLE[type & 0x0f] (0..15 reachable).
verdict.fix_summary: The claim is correct and survives refutation. Byte-decode of 0x418..0x42f is exactly {10,20,1000,1000,1000,50,100,200,300,1000,0,0} (12 s16). Entries 12-15 {771,2569,3595,4111} are byte-true the adjacent DAT_8006f430 array misread as s16: 0x0303=771, 0x0a09=2569, 0x0e0b=3595, 0x100f=4111 — all match 03 03 09 0a 0b 0e 0f 10. DAT_8006f430 is a DISTINCT array (own XREF, accessed as *(undefined1*) byte vs *(short*) for 0x418), so the 16-wide declaration is wrong. Critically, our :106 masks `type & 0x0f` (0..15), so any type with low nibble 12-15 reads garbage damage; the original masks `& 0xff` against a 12-entry table relying on no type>=12 ever being passed. Fix: `static const int16_t DMG_TABLE[12] = {10,20,1000,1000,1000,50,100,200,300,1000,0,0};` and change the index mask to a guarded `type` (0..11) — e.g. clamp/reject index>=12 — instead of `& 0x0f`, to avoid out-of-bounds reads.

claim: `DMG_TABLE[type & 0x0f]` masks the type to 4 bits. The original indexes by `param_3 & 0xff` (full byte). The 4-bit mask wraps types 16..255 into 0..15 instead of the byte the EXE uses, and combined with the bogus indices 12-15 in the over-sized table can return garbage. Cosmetic in practice (callers pass 0/1) but provably wrong masking width.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:77671 (DAT_8006f418 + v1, where v1 derived from param_3 byte); FUN_80012d60.c:38 *(short*)(&DAT_8006f418 + (param_3 & 0xff) * 2) and FUN_80012d60.c:80 + param_3 * 2
fix: Use `DMG_TABLE[type & 0xff]` against the 12-entry table with a bounds guard (the EXE relies on callers only emitting valid indices 0-9). Keep the byte mask to match the original index width.

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:77664 (`80012e38 andi a0,s7,0xff`) then 77665 (`sll v1,a0,0x1`) + 77671 (`lhu v1,0x0(at)=>DAT_8006f418`) prove the index mask is `& 0xff`, not `& 0x0f`. FUN_80012d60.c:38 (`(param_3 & 0xff) * 2`) and :61/:80 (`param_3 & 0xff` then `param_3 * 2`) confirm in C. Table size: ghidra1_V2.txt:223455-223478 shows DAT_8006f418 spans 8006f418..8006f42f = 12 s16 entries {0A,14,03E8,03E8,03E8,32,64,C8,12C,03E8,00,00}; the very next symbol DAT_8006f430 starts at 223479/8006f430 — so our DMG_TABLE[16] entries 12-15 (771,2569,3595,4111) are garbage reinterpreting the DAT_8006f430 clip-id bytes (03 03 09 0a 0b 0e 0f 10). The original load (77779/77782) has NO bounds check before indexing.
verdict.fix_summary: REAL deviation (could not refute): player_damage_common.c:106 masks `type & 0x0f`; EXE masks `& 0xff` (insn 80012e38). Byte-true fix: change `& 0x0f` to `& 0xff`. Also fix the oversized table — the EXE damage table is only 12 s16 entries (8006f418..8006f42f, next symbol DAT_8006f430); shrink DMG_TABLE to {10,20,1000,1000,1000,50,100,200,300,1000,0,0} (our entries 12-15 are garbage read from the adjacent clip-id table). NOTE the prior audit's proposed fix is wrong on one point: do NOT add a bounds guard — the EXE has none (raw index at 80012e54/80012ff4, no range check), it trusts caller-supplied indices 0-9. A guard would itself be non-byte-true. Severity is cosmetic/latent: `re15_player_damage` is currently never called (zombie bite uses re15_player_grab), and live callers would pass 0/1, so neither the mask width nor the oversized tail is exercised today — but the masking width is provably wrong, so it qualifies as a deviation to fix.

claim: PLY_HIT_FRAMES=24 and PLY_DEATH_FRAMES=44 are admitted-tunable fixed frame counts driving the i-frame/state duration. The original ends the hit-react (and clears the bit0 latch) on the react CLIP's own keyframe wrap, not a fixed counter. So both the i-frame window length and the HIT->NONE transition are heuristic, not byte-true.
disasm_cite: FUN_80012d60.c:51 sets DAT_800acae7|=1 (latch) with no duration here; the clear happens at clip end in the state-2 handler region (ghidra1_V2.txt clears at 0x800362fc/0x80036420/0x80036690 sb zero,DAT_800acae7) driven by the clip, not a frame constant. The exact clip length lives in PL00.edd keyframe count (not a literal in the EXE).
fix: Drive the HIT/DEATH end from the actual react-clip keyframe wrap (use the EDD clip length for the queued react motion) instead of fixed 24/44. Until the clip-frame plumbing exists, flag as approximation; cite the EDD length once wired.

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80012d60.c:51 (sb DAT_800acae7|=1, NO duration set at damage entry); ghidra1_V2.txt:242022 (state[2]=HIT->LAB_80035af0) + :131888 (HIT dispatches on DAT_800aca59=react-dir+2 into PTR_LAB_800741a8) + :132422 (0x800362fc sb zero,DAT_800acae7 in sub-case 4 of the react-anim machine LAB_800360e8, gated by root-motion counter DAT_800acae0 init 0x3e8 @:132314 / collision flag DAT_800acaf2 @:132363, NOT a frame literal); ghidra1_V2.txt:242023 (state[3]=DEATH->LAB_800366bc) + :132776-132780 (death sub-state advances DAT_800aca5a += FUN_8001f314 return = animation-pose-driven) + :132786-132788 (state->7 game-over only after the death sequence completes). Our deviating code: psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/player_damage_common.c:35-36 (PLY_HIT_FRAMES=24, PLY_DEATH_FRAMES=44, comment admits "EXE ends these on the clip's own keyframe wrap ... use fixed frame counts - tunable"), :145 (++s_timer>=PLY_HIT_FRAMES -> PLY_NONE) and :207 (++s_timer>=PLY_DEATH_FRAMES -> GAMEOVER).
verdict.fix_summary: REAL deviation, NOT engine-specific. Verified the cite: the EXE has no 24/44 literal in the hit/death path. The i-frame latch DAT_800acae7 is set bit0 at damage entry (FUN_80012d60.c:51) with no duration, and cleared only when the react-animation sub-state machine completes: HIT handler LAB_80035af0/LAB_800360e8 clears it in sub-case 4 once the root-motion knockback counter DAT_800acae0 (init 0x3e8, decremented ~15/frame) exhausts and the react clip wraps (ghidra1_V2.txt:132422); DEATH handler LAB_800366bc/FUN_80036718 advances its sub-state by FUN_8001f314's animation return and only flips to game-over (state 7) when the death clip sequence finishes (ghidra1_V2.txt:132776-132788). So both PLY_HIT_FRAMES=24 and PLY_DEATH_FRAMES=44 are heuristics, and the i-frame window length (re15_player_invulnerable gated on s_iframe>0) is wrong too. Byte-true fix: drive the HIT->NONE and DEATH->GAMEOVER transitions (and i-frame clear) off the queued react/death clip's keyframe wrap. The plumbing the prior audit wanted to wait for ALREADY EXISTS: re15_actor.h exposes frame_count with an explicit "cur >= frame_count = ended" distinction and re15_emd.h parses per-clip frame_count from the EDD clip table. So end the state when the actor's current keyframe index for s_hit_clip/PLY_CLIP_DEATH reaches that clip's frame_count, instead of fixed 24/44. (Only refinement of the audit's framing: this is a confirmed deviation, not 'uncertain' - it's actionable now, the EDD length is available; the exact frame value just isn't an EXE literal because it lives in PL00.edd.)

claim: Knockback PLY_KB_STEP=33 over PLY_KB_FRAMES=6 (~198, comment claims 'byte-true 0xc8'=200) using a unit-vector-away-from-attacker pushback. I could NOT find 0xc8 or the header-comment 0x1428 react-velocity in the HIT-react region. FUN_80012d60 sets only state + react clip + HP; no position knockback is applied there. The knockback magnitude/mechanism is uncited — a heuristic.

If a recoil counter is later wired to drive stagger timing, model DAT_800acae0 (init 0xc8 ONLY in handler slots de0/f64, decay DAT_800acaf2=0x32) — but the b70/ca8 path used by DAT_800aca59=2/3 has counter=0, so for the standard bite hit the byte-true behavior is: stagger clip 0xa, no knockback. Delete the unproven '0xc8'/'0x1428' comments.

claim: PLY_EATEN_FRAMES=50 is a tuned fixed frame count for the eaten clip before game-over. The original does NOT count a fixed number of frames: the DEATH/EATEN state advances its substate only when the animation clip WRAPS (clip-done), which it derives from the clip's own keyframe table, not a constant.

Same for PLY_DEATH_FRAMES=44. The real length = number of keyframes in PL00 clip 0x06 / clip 7 (read from the loaded EDD), not a magic constant.

verdict.fix_summary: Replace the fixed PLY_DEATH_FRAMES=44 / PLY_EATEN_FRAMES=50 timers with the clip-wrap signal. FUN_80036718 advances the death/eaten substate by FUN_8001f314's bool return, which is true only when the player's keyframe index ([0x95]) reaches the clip's own keyframe count ([0x94]-clip header low-16) and wraps (FUN_8001f8b4.c:78-82). re15_actor_set_motion already binds the clip; have the actor anim-step expose a 'clip wrapped this frame' flag (kf_idx>=kf_count) and gate the PLY_DEATH->GAMEOVER and PLY_EATEN->GAMEOVER transitions on it instead of '++s_timer >= constant'. Same applies to the death fall (clip 0x06/7). 44 and 50 are not the real keyframe counts (50 was a savestate-observed playback duration, not the EDD keyframe-table count).

claim: The PLY_GAMEOVER (state 7) handler is a bare stub that just freezes ('The EXE state-7 red screen-fade / game-over menu is a follow-up.'). The original state-7 handler runs a concrete red screen-fade: it seeds a 0x78 (=120) frame hold counter, sets the fade overlay color, and ramps two fade values by +0xc each frame, then advances to a done substate.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:132872-132917 (LAB_8003694c): 132885 ori v0,0x78 -> DAT_800acaf2 (=120 fade frames); 132877-132900 masks DAT_800acb18/DAT_800acb40 with 0xff000000 and ORs 0x0000ff38 (fade color); 132902-132912 each frame DAT_800acb10/DAT_800acb12 += 0xc and DAT_800acaf2-- ; 132913-132914 when counter hits 0 -> DAT_800aca59 = 2 (fade complete)
fix: Implement the state-7 fade: on entering GAMEOVER seed a 120-frame counter, apply the red fade overlay (color low-word 0xff38, +0xc/frame ramp on the two fade accumulators), decrement the counter each frame, and mark fade-complete (sub-state 2) at 0. This is the byte-true eaten->game-over red fade currently missing.

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:132885 (ori v0,0x78 -> sb DAT_800acaf2 = 120 fade frames @132888); ghidra1_V2.txt:132883-132884,132893-132900 (fade color: lui a1,0xff / ori a1,0xff38; DAT_800acb18 & 0xff000000 | 0xff38, same for DAT_800acb40); ghidra1_V2.txt:132902-132909 (DAT_800acb10 += 0xc, DAT_800acb12 += 0xc each frame); ghidra1_V2.txt:132910-132914 (counter-- ; when 0 -> DAT_800aca59 = 2 done). State-7 mapping proven: ghidra1_V2.txt:132786-132788 (LAB_80036804 sets DAT_800aca58=7) routes the death sequence into LAB_8003694c; the dispatcher FUN_80031c44:127085-127093 indexes PTR_LAB_80073f90 by DAT_800aca58 and entry idx7 (242027) = LAB_8003694c; and the damage entry FUN_80012d60:77709-77712 sets DAT_800aca58=3 (=our PLY_DEATH=3) when HP(DAT_800acaee)<0, confirming the enum is byte-identical to our s_state (PLY_GAMEOVER=7). Our code: psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/player_damage_common.c:210-212 PLY_GAMEOVER case is a bare 'return 1' stub; grep confirms no game-over red fade exists anywhere in either port.
verdict.fix_summary: Implement the byte-true state-7 red fade in the PLY_GAMEOVER handler. On first entry (substate 0->1) seed a 120-frame (0x78) hold counter (DAT_800acaf2) and set the fade overlay color: take the two overlay color words (DAT_800acb18, DAT_800acb40), mask with 0xff000000 and OR in the low word 0xff38 (yielding 0xff00ff38 = red-tinted fade). Each frame thereafter ramp the two fade accumulators DAT_800acb10 and DAT_800acb12 by +0xc, decrement the counter; when it reaches 0 set the fade-complete substate (DAT_800aca59 = 2). Bind the two ramped accumulators + overlay color into whatever screen-overlay the port already uses (PSX GPU fill vs PC/SDL overlay quad) so the eaten/death sequence ends on the red game-over fade currently missing.

Clip 0x06 is the GRAB-EATEN clip (savestate st6/7), which is a different state (5->6), so the plain-death collapse should be clip 7.

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80036718.c:16 (DAT_800acae8 = 7) — verified in raw disasm ghidra1_V2.txt:132747-132749 (ori v1,zero,0x7 ; sb v1,-0x3518(at)=>DAT_800acae8). FUN_80036718 IS the state-3 DEATH handler: dispatch table PTR_LAB_80073f90 (ghidra1_V2.txt:242019-242023) index 3 = 80073f9c = LAB_800366bc, and LAB_800366bc tail-calls FUN_80036718 (ghidra1_V2.txt:132706). verdict.fix_summary: Change PLY_CLIP_DEATH from 0x06 to 7 (player_damage_common.c:30), used by the PLY_DEATH state at line 203 — the state-3 DEATH handler writes DAT_800acae8=7 (FUN_80036718.c:16 / disasm 132749). Keep PLY_CLIP_EATEN=0x06 unchanged for the grab-driven PLY_EATEN state (5->6, savestate st6/7 confirmed), which is the path the room1140 zombie grab actually uses. Net: plain non-grab death (HP<0 from a normal hit) collapses with clip 7; grab-eaten death keeps clip 6.

claim: The escape GATE is a fabricated button-mash counter: `PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_MAX 100`, `re15_player_grab_struggle()` accumulating mash ticks, and kick-off fired when `s_grab_struggle >= PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_MAX`. The audit FOCUS asked to verify this against FUN_80011f50:146 ("kick flag when struggle reaches type-7") — but FUN_80011f50 is the HIT-DETECTION/damage function (callers 0x80012418=damage entry, 0x80033554; ghidra1_V2.txt:75820). Its `param_1` is the ATTACK/hit TYPE, not a struggle counter. Line 146 sets target flag 0x40 when `param_1 == 7` (a grab-type hit) OR (`param_1 == 8` && dist<3000). There is no counter, no value 100, and no accumulation anywhere. The real break-free handler FUN_8011a1c8 contains NO pad read (func_0x800245d8 is absent, unlike FUN_8011a9ec:89) and is gated by animation-substate player[0x95]==0x0f plus the range test func_0x8001a780, not by mashing.
disasm_cite: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80011f50.c:146 (`if (((param_1 == 8) && (DAT_8008f5e0 < 3000)) || (param_1 == 7))` -> flag |0x40); RE_15_Quellcode_Overlays/STAGE1/FUN_8011a1c8.c:39,53 (`if (*(char *)(_DAT_800ac784 + 0x95) != '\x0f') return;` = the escape gate)
fix: Remove PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_MAX/decay and the face-button struggle ticks (game_step_common.c:47-51). Drive the break-free FSM by player[0x95] substate progression exactly like FUN_8011a1c8: the grab clip player[0x94]=0x1c plays, player[0x95] advances via the anim driver (func_0x8001f314,…,0x200), and when player[0x95] reaches 0x0f the engine runs the range check func_0x8001a780(player-target); on success it transitions to player[6]=7 / player[0x95]=0x23 (the kick) and fires func_0x800453d0(7). No mashing input exists; escape is anim-time + range gated.

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80011f50.c:146 (`if (((param_1 == 8) && (DAT_8008f5e0 < 3000)) || (param_1 == 7))` sets target flag |0x40 by HIT TYPE — no counter/100/accumulation); ghidra1_V2.txt:75820 + :76158/:76159 (`jal FUN_80011f50` / `_move a0,s0` where s0 = hit-type byte from `andi s0,s8,0xff` @ghidra1_V2.txt:76132, so param_1 is the attack TYPE not a struggle counter; 9 caller xrefs 80012418/80033554/... are all damage sites); RE_15_Quellcode_Overlays/STAGE1/FUN_8011a1c8.c:39 (`if (*(char *)(_DAT_800ac784 + 0x95) != '\x0f') return;` = the break-free gate), :42 (range test func_0x8001a780), :56-57 (player[6]=7 / player[0x95]=0x23 = kick) with NO pad read — contrast FUN_8011a9ec.c:89,93 which DOES call pad func_0x800245d8. Our deviation: psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/player_damage_common.c:50,84,170 + game_step_common.c:47-51 (face-button mash feeds s_grab_struggle, kick at >=100).
verdict.fix_summary: Real deviation, confirmed. The cite is accurate: FUN_80011f50 is the hit-detection/damage function (param_1 = attack TYPE, verified at the caller 80012418 via `_move a0,s0` with s0 = type byte), and line 146 raises a target flag purely by hit type — there is no struggle counter, no value 100, and no accumulation anywhere in the EXE. The true break-free handler FUN_8011a1c8 contains NO pad read and is gated by anim substate player[0x95] reaching 0x0f plus the range test func_0x8001a780, then transitions player[6]=7/player[0x95]=0x23 (kick) and fires func_0x800453d0(7). Our PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_MAX 100 + face-button mash ticks are fabricated. Fix byte-true: remove PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_MAX/decay and the struggle ticks in game_step_common.c:47-51; drive break-free off the grab clip's anim substate — play grab clip player[0x94]=0x1c, advance player[0x95] via the anim driver (func_0x8001f314,...,0x200), and when player[0x95] reaches 0x0f run func_0x8001a780(player-target); on success set player[6]=7/player[0x95]=0x23 (kick) and call func_0x800453d0(7). Escape = anim-time + range gated, never input-mash gated.

claim: The step-back distance/duration is tuned magic (45 units/frame for 14 frames). The original does not push by a fixed per-frame delta; FUN_8011a1c8 calls the engine move/approach helpers with literal magnitudes 3000 and 800 toward the player target DAT_800aca88, and the kick step-back is driven by clip 0x1c + func_0x8001a804(3000,0x100,&DAT_800aca88). Neither 45 nor 14 is cited.
disasm_cite: RE_15_Quellcode_Overlays/STAGE1/FUN_8011a1c8.c:31 (`func_0x8001a804(3000,0x100,&DAT_800aca88);`) and :48 (`func_0x8001bff8(...,800,&DAT_800aca88);`)
fix: Replace the fixed PLY_KICK_STEP/PLY_KICK_FRAMES push with the cited move magnitudes (3000 with limit 0x100 via the a804 approach helper relative to the player target), and end the kick on clip-0x1c animation wrap rather than a 14-frame timer. Needs func_0x8001a804/8001bff8 decompiled or a DuckStation RAM trace to capture the exact integration before finalizing byte-true.

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_Overlays/STAGE1/FUN_8011a1c8.c:18 (player[0x94]=0x1c -> kick-off clip 0x1c), :31 (func_0x8001a804(3000,0x100,&DAT_800aca88) at anim-frame 0x95==4), :48 (func_0x8001bff8(...,800,&DAT_800aca88) at anim-frame 0x95==0xf); caller RE_15_Quellcode_Overlays/STAGE1/FUN_80119770.c:25-27 + :47 (func_0x8001a804(5000,0x180,&DAT_800aca88)) confirm the player struggle/kick FSM drives motion via move-to-target helpers, not a fixed per-frame delta. Our code: psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/player_damage_common.c:52-53 (PLY_KICK_FRAMES 14 / PLY_KICK_STEP 45, self-labelled "tunable") and :183-186 (plays PLY_CLIP_WALK 0x0c and pushes p->x+=s_kb_x for 14 frames).
verdict.fix_summary: Real deviation, but only partially fixable from the cites today. The original kick-off (FUN_8011a1c8) plays clip 0x1c (player[0x94]=0x1c, FUN_8011a1c8.c:18) and, keyed to that clip's own animation frames (+0x95), drives the player toward target DAT_800aca88 via the move helpers func_0x8001a804(3000,0x100,&tgt) at frame 4 and func_0x8001bff8(...,800,&tgt) at frame 0xf, then ends to state[6]=2 (recover) or 7 (game-over). Our PLY_KICK_STEP=45 / PLY_KICK_FRAMES=14 fixed push with the WALK clip 0x0c is a tuned placeholder that matches neither the clip (should be 0x1c) nor the motion model. Byte-true fix: (1) play clip 0x1c not 0x0c; (2) replace the 45-units/14-frames integration with a move-toward-DAT_800aca88 step using magnitudes 3000(limit 0x100) and 800 gated on the clip-0x1c keyframes; (3) end on the clip wrap, not a 14-frame timer. BLOCKER: func_0x8001a804 and func_0x8001bff8 are NOT yet decompiled (no FUN_8001a804.c/FUN_8001bff8.c in RE_15_Quellcode_V2), so the exact per-frame world-delta conversion of 3000/0x100/800 must first be obtained by decompiling those two helpers or a DuckStation RAM trace before the magnitudes can be made truly byte-true. So: confirm the clip (0x1c) and the move-to-target model now; finalize the numeric magnitudes after the helpers are RE'd.

claim: The '34 per face-button press, ~3 presses = free' and the bleed-down decay (player_damage_common.c:51,163) are pure tuning with no disasm basis. They exist only to satisfy the fabricated 100-counter; the original grab escape reads no pad input at all.
disasm_cite: RE_15_Quellcode_Overlays/STAGE1/FUN_8011a1c8.c:25-43 (entire escape path: gated by player[0x95] substate + func_0x8001a780 range test; no func_0x800245d8 pad read present)
fix: Delete the pad-driven struggle accumulation in game_step_common.c:42-51 and the decay PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_DECAY. The grab outcome (eaten vs broken-free) is decided by the engine's range/anim FSM, not player input — the reference savestates show Leon eaten with no input dependency.

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_Overlays/STAGE1/FUN_8011a1c8.c:25,33,39,42,51,56-57 — the grab escape FSM: kick-off path gated on player[0x95]==0x04 (line 25) + the angle test func_0x8001a780 (lines 26,42); eaten path on player[0x95]==0x0f (lines 39,53) setting state player[6]=7 & player[0x95]=0x23 (lines 56-57). Substate player[0x95] is advanced only by the anim-step func_0x8001f314 (line 23). func_0x8001a780 = RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8001a780.c:4-5: a pure facing-angle range test ((entity[0x6a]-player[0x6a]+0x400)&0xfff)<0x800 — NO pad input. Verified NONE of the 9 functions in the player-grab state group (FUN_80119198/80119438/801199f8/80119c4c/8011a1c8/8011a43c/8011a5f8/8011a75c/8011aefc, all dispatched via table -0x7fedeb58/-0x7fedeb00) reference the pad-read call OR the pad globals DAT_800ac76c/DAT_800ac768 (grep count 0 each). Contrast our pad-mash mechanic at psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/game_step_common.c:47-51 and player_damage_common.c:50-51,163,170. (Note: the finding's id of func_0x800245d8 as the pad-read is imprecise — FUN_800245d8 is actually a MATRIX setup routine — but the core claim holds: no pad mechanism of any kind exists in the grab FSM.)
verdict.fix_summary: Delete the pad-driven escape entirely. Remove the grab-struggle accumulation block in game_step_common.c:47-51 (the re15_player_grab_struggle(34) call) and the PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_MAX/PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_DECAY counter logic in player_damage_common.c (the s_grab_struggle field, lines 50-51, 65, 83-84, 163, and the `s_grab_struggle >= MAX -> kick-off` branch at 170-179). Replace the escape decision with the byte-true engine FSM from FUN_8011a1c8: the grab substate advances via the animation step, and the kick-off-vs-eaten branch is the facing-angle range test FUN_8001a780 (((grabber[+0x6a]-player[+0x6a]+0x400)&0xfff)<0x800) evaluated when the struggle anim reaches substate 0x04, while the eaten path fires at substate 0x0f. No player input gates the outcome — port the substate-byte + angle-test FSM, not a mash counter. The reference savestates (Leon eaten with no input dependency) corroborate this.

claim: BACK_SPEED_PER_FRAME = 60 (0x3C). The real free-roam back-walk handler reads the backward speed byte DAT_80073ec4 = 0x46 = 70 into the actor speed field (+0x8c / DAT_800acae0), not 60. The cited basis in our comment ('FUN_80041BE4 mode tables ... BACK=0x3C(60)') is a SCD/cinematic table, not the free-roam pad handler that actually drives g_actors[0] every tick.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:128265 (lbu a0,DAT_80073ec4 = 46h) + ghidra1_V2.txt:128284 (sh a0,DAT_800acae0) in handler LAB_80032d20; table value at ghidra1_V2.txt:241860 (80073ec4 = 46h)
fix: #define BACK_SPEED_PER_FRAME 70  /* 0x46 — DAT_80073ec4, free-roam back handler LAB_80032d20 */

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:128265 (lbu a0,DAT_80073ec4 = 46h) + ghidra1_V2.txt:128284 (sh a0,DAT_800acae0) in player-FSM dispatch handler LAB_80032d20 (jump-table entry ghidra1_V2.txt:242050, table 0x80073ff0); back-speed table byte at ghidra1_V2.txt:241860 (80073ec4=46h, in the 0x46-region of the speed/turn table starting 80073ea4=4B/30=WALK). INDEPENDENT corroboration in the SECOND table family our own comment cites (FUN_80041BE4 / 0x80076cXX): ghidra1_V2.txt:252761-252767 (DAT_80076c08..0e = 46h = the back-walk slots) and ghidra1_V2.txt:177816 (lbu ...=>DAT_80076c20 = 46h). Refutation attempt: grep for any 0x3C byte in 80076c[0-3]x returned NO matches — the value 60 our code uses exists in NEITHER table. WALK=0x4B(75) at 252751/177302 and RUN=0xC8(200) at 177521 both match our code exactly; only BACK deviates.
verdict.fix_summary: Change psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/player_common.c:32 from `#define BACK_SPEED_PER_FRAME 60` to `#define BACK_SPEED_PER_FRAME 70  /* 0x46 — back-walk speed, DAT_80073ec4 (player-FSM handler LAB_80032d20, ghidra1_V2.txt:128265) and DAT_80076c08 (FUN_80041BE4 mode table). NOT 0x3C. */`. Also correct the misleading comment at lines 27-29: the cited FUN_80041BE4 / 0x80076cXX table holds BACK=0x46(70), never 0x3C(60); the 60 was a fabricated value. WALK=75 and RUN=200 are already byte-true and need no change. Since this is a shared #define, the PC port (re15_reborn_pc) inherits the fix via the same header/source — verify it isn't separately overridden.

claim: BACK locomotion clip + direction mechanism are port-invented. Our port plays the WALK clip (RE15_MOTION_BACK = RE15_MOTION_WALK = 105 = W01 clip 5) with RE15_ANIM_REVERSE set, and reverses translation by negating move_dir (cos*speed*-1). The original free-roam back handler (LAB_80032d20) sets motion clip 0 (DAT_800acae8 = 0, the RUN clip — written via 'sb zero,acae8'), sets NO reverse flag, and produces the backward translation by passing a 180-deg yaw OFFSET (param_1 = 0x800) to the mover FUN_800245d8 (which rotates {speed,0,0} by RotMatrixY(heading + 0x800)). Net position direction is the same, but the rendered clip (0 vs 5) and the lack of a reverse-anim flag differ.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:128275 (sb zero,DAT_800acae8 => clip 0) + ghidra1_V2.txt:128286-128287 (jal FUN_800245d8 with _ori a0,zero,0x800) in LAB_80032d20; mover math FUN_800245d8 line 33 (RotMatrixY(actor+0x6a + param_1)) in RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_800245d8.c
fix: Map BACK to motion clip 0 (run clip) with NO reverse flag, and implement the backward step as heading+0x800 (180deg) rather than negating move_dir / setting RE15_ANIM_REVERSE. i.e. for move_dir<0: speed=70, no reverse flag, motion=RE15_MOTION_RUN, and add the move vector rotated by (rot_y + 2048).

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:128275 (80032d84 `sb zero,-0x3518(at)=>DAT_800acae8` => BACK handler LAB_80032d20 sets clip selector = 0, NOT 5) + ghidra1_V2.txt:128286-128287 (80032dac `jal FUN_800245d8` / 80032db0 `_ori a0,zero,0x800` => backward via 180deg heading offset, no reverse flag) + RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_800245d8.c:33 (`RotMatrixY((int)*(short *)(DAT_800ac784 + 0x6a) + (int)param_1,&local_28)` => rotates the {speed,0,0} step by heading+param). Contrast forward-walk handler LAB_80032498 ghidra1_V2.txt:127670 (`sb v0(=5),...=>DAT_800acae8` clip 5) and its mover call ghidra1_V2.txt:127741 (`jal FUN_800245d8` with `_clear a0`, offset 0). Dispatch table ghidra1_V2.txt:242046-242050 (0x80073ff0: idx0=IDLE LAB_80032038 ... idx4=LAB_80032d20); LAB_80032d20 is the ONLY locomotion mover passing a 0x800 (180deg) offset => the unique backward handler. Our deviating code: psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/player_common.c:65 (`#define RE15_MOTION_BACK RE15_MOTION_WALK` =105=clip5), :66 (`RE15_ANIM_REVERSE 0x80` port-invented), :189 (BACK uses WALK clip), :251 negated move_dir, :256 (`p->anim_flags |= RE15_ANIM_REVERSE`).
verdict.fix_summary: Real byte-true deviation, NOT refuted. Original free-roam BACK handler (LAB_80032d20, dispatch mode 4) plays animation CLIP 0 from the back/common bank (DAT_800acad8/DAT_800acbc0 via FUN_8001f314 @128301-128309), sets NO reverse-playback flag, and produces backward translation by passing a 180-degree (0x800) heading offset to the mover FUN_800245d8 — which rotates the {speed,0,0} step vector by RotMatrixY(actor_heading[+0x6a] + 0x800). Our port instead reuses the WALK clip (105=clip 5, RE15_MOTION_BACK aliased to RE15_MOTION_WALK), invents an RE15_ANIM_REVERSE (0x80) flag that has no counterpart in the original animation system, and reverses translation by negating move_dir (cos*speed*-1). Net world-position direction is identical, but the RENDERED CLIP (0 vs 5) and the (nonexistent-in-original) reverse-anim flag both differ — a mechanism deviation, not an accepted RE2/SDL-arch difference. FIX: map BACK to motion clip 0 with NO reverse flag, and step via heading+0x800 (180deg) instead of negating move_dir / setting the reverse flag. Caveat: only the clip-index + flag + 0x800-offset core is disasm-proven here; the BACK movement SPEED constant (audit's 'speed=70') and the assertion 'clip 0 == the run clip' were not independently re-derived in this pass — verify the back-speed byte and clip-0 identity before finalizing the visual.

claim: The SCRIPTED (Plc_dest/Plc_motion) walker constants are byte-true against the cinematic mode handler LAB_80030af0: walk step 0x4B(75), yaw-slew-init 0x15E (FUN_8001ab9c a2), state-2 slew 0x60 then 0x30 (FUN_8001aac4 a2), Euclidean arrival radius 0x64=100. This file is a different system from player_common.c (it drives walk_active dest-seeking, not the pad), so its constants are correctly separated.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:125380 (ori v0,0x4b walk step), ghidra1_V2.txt:125408 (_ori a2,0x15e -> FUN_8001ab9c), ghidra1_V2.txt:125417 (_ori a2,0x60 -> FUN_8001aac4) & ghidra1_V2.txt:125465 (_ori a2,0x30), ghidra1_V2.txt:125492 (slti v0,v0,0x64 arrival)
fix: none

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:125407-125408 (jal FUN_8001ab9c, _ori a2,0x15e) + FUN_8001ab9c.c:10-16 — 0x15e is the CONVERGENCE/transition gate (returns 0 when |delta|<=0x15e, advancing state 1->2), it does NOT move yaw. ghidra1_V2.txt:125416-125417 (jal FUN_8001aac4, _ori a2,0x60) + FUN_8001aac4.c:7-22 — FUN_8001aac4 is the actual state-1 yaw SLEW, rotating actor+0x6a toward target by +/-0x60 per tick. Our actor_locomotion.c:231-234 instead slews state-1 by up to PLC_YAW_SLEW_INIT=0x15e/tick (s1 clamp added straight to rot_y) AND reuses 0x15e as PLC_CONV_THRESHOLD — conflating the slew rate with the transition gate.
verdict.fix_summary: The original state-1 ALIGN phase slews yaw by 0x60/tick (FUN_8001aac4 a2=0x60, ghidra1_V2.txt:125417), and uses 0x15e ONLY as the state-1->2 convergence gate (FUN_8001ab9c a2=0x15e, ghidra1_V2.txt:125408). Our code (actor_locomotion.c:231-234) wrongly slews state-1 by up to 0x15e/tick. Fix: introduce a distinct PLC_YAW_SLEW_S1=0x60 and clamp s1 to +/-0x60 (the slew), while keeping PLC_CONV_THRESHOLD=0x15e ONLY for the state-1->2 transition test (line 234). All other cited constants (0x4b walk speed via FUN_800245d8 actor+0x8c, 0x30 state-2 walk slew, 0x48 state-2 run slew, 0x64/0x12c arrival radii) ARE byte-true. NOTE: the in-code memory comment (lines 83-93) already flags this as USER-A/B-tested 'visually neutral' for the sub02 cutscene and the project RULE keeps the byte-true value — so byte-true here means restoring 0x60 for the slew, which is the original. Either way the current 0x15e slew is the non-byte-true one.

claim: On a scripted walk/run ARRIVAL our code PINS the clip at frame 0 forever (anim_freeze→slot=0). The original player anim processor NEVER freezes a frame: at clip-end it WRAPS the frame counter to 0 and keeps LOOPING (both terminal handlers do this). Our comment claims 'FUN_8001f3bc wraps the frame counter to 0 + freezes there' but the disasm shows wrap WITHOUT freeze — the clip would keep marching. The 'hold frame 0' is a port invention substituting for the unmodelled post-arrival player FSM state.
disasm_cite: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8001f3bc.c:89-94 (bVar3=*(+0x95)+1; if(frame_count<=bVar3) *(+0x95)=0 — wrap-and-continue, no freeze); RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8001f8b4.c:76-81 (the 0x8000-variant also only wraps +0x95 to 0); ghidra1_V2.txt:125492-125511 (WALK arrival branch writes ONLY the player FSM state DAT_800aca59=6/4 + DAT_800aca5a — nothing to actor motion/frame/flags)
fix: The real behavior depends on the post-arrival player FSM handler PTR_LAB_80073e30[6] (=DAT_800aca59=6), which is in the undisassembled overlay region (0x80050cb8 area shows as raw ?? in ghidra1_V2.txt:176772+). RE that state-6 handler (Ghidra headless on the overlay) to learn whether it switches to an idle clip or holds the walk-stand; only then can the freeze-at-slot-0 be confirmed/replaced byte-true. Until then this is a heuristic, not a citation-backed match.

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:241787-241790 (dispatch table PTR_LAB_80073e30: index[6]=80073e48 -> LAB_800517f0, NOT 0x80050cb8); ghidra1_V2.txt:177722-177799 (state-6 handler LAB_800517f0 IS fully disassembled); ghidra1_V2.txt:177746-177758 (LAB_80051844: on entry it WRITES motion +0x94=1, frame +0x95=0, blend +0x8f=7 -> sub-state+0x6=1); ghidra1_V2.txt:177776-177785 (LAB_800518b4: next phase writes motion +0x94=2, blend +0x8f=7); ghidra1_V2.txt:125502-125511 (WALK arrival sets entity+0x5=DAT_800aca59=6 and entity+0x6=DAT_800aca5a=0); ghidra1_V2.txt:77704,77707 (proves DAT_800aca59=entity+0x5, DAT_800aca5a=entity+0x6); FUN_8001f3bc.c:89-93 (frame +0x95 wrap-to-0); our anim_select_common.c:43-44 (anim_freeze->slot 0) + actor_locomotion.c:315-333 (sets anim_freeze=1)
verdict.fix_summary:
A real deviation exists, but the prior audit MIS-DESCRIBED both the original behavior and the fix, so its specific claim is refuted: (1) FALSE "keeps the walk clip LOOPING" — the original does NOT loop the walk clip on arrival. (2) FALSE "state-6 handler is undisassembled at 0x80050cb8 ??" — the dispatch index for state 6 is PTR_LAB_80073e30[6]=80073e48->LAB_800517f0 (ghidra1_V2.txt:241789), which IS fully disassembled at 177722. The audit confused index[0] (=80050cb8, line 241783) with index[6].

The ACTUAL byte-true post-WALK-arrival behavior: arrival writes entity+0x5(state)=6, +0x6(sub)=0 (125506-125511). Next tick the FSM dispatch (8003068c: PTR_LAB_80073e30 + state*4) runs LAB_800517f0, a settle sub-FSM on entity+0x6: sub0 -> motion(+0x94)=1, frame(+0x95)=0, blend(+0x8f)=7, sub->1 (177750-177758); sub1 -> run/blend FUN_8001f314; sub2 -> motion=2, blend=7 (177781); sub3 -> blend. So the player CROSSFADES walk -> motion 1 -> motion 2 (PL00 idle-settle clips), it does NOT hold the walk clip at frame 0 and does NOT loop it.

Our code (anim_freeze -> slot 0) pins the walk clip at frame 0 indefinitely until the next Plc_motion. That genuinely deviates from the original motion-1->motion-2 settle. byte-true fix: on Plc_dest WALK/RUN arrival, drive the entity+0x5=6 / +0x6=0 settle FSM (emit motion 1 then motion 2 with a 7-frame +0x8f crossfade and frame reset) instead of setting anim_freeze=1; map motions 1/2 to the PL00 settle clips. The state-6 handler is in-EXE (LAB_800517f0) so this needs NO overlay RE — contrary to the audit's "proposed fix".

claim: During the state-4 init tick our code holds anim_frame at 0 AND does NOT decrement anim_frac (preserving the frac=7 first-blend frame). The frame-hold matches the documented state=4→state=1 1-tick FSM, but the 'hold frac during init' decision cannot be verified: the state-4 player FSM handler lives in the overlay region that this Ghidra export did not disassemble.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:176772-176831 (the 0x80050cb8/0x80050cdc FSM region is emitted as raw ?? bytes — not analyzed); RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8001f3bc.c:78 shows frac (+0x8f) is decremented INSIDE the anim processor only when uVar5!=0, but whether the processor is even called during the state-4 init frame is unknown from this export.
fix: Run Ghidra headless on the player-FSM overlay (per CLAUDE.md: analyzeHeadless on the relevant BIN / re-export 0x80050000 region) to disassemble the state-4 handler and confirm whether the anim processor runs (and thus whether frac decrements) on the init frame. If it does run, our frac-hold over-preserves one blend frame.

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:176956-176996 (LAB_80050cb8, the REAL player-anim FSM — the prior audit wrongly claimed this was un-disassembled). State byte at +0x6: 176961-176968 dispatch (0=init, 1=advance, >=2=frozen). Init path 176970-176987: 80050cec sb state=1; 80050cf8/cfc ori 0x7 / sb +0x8f=7 (seed frac); 80050d0c sb zero,+0x95 (frame=0); then FALLS THROUGH at 80050d30→176988 LAB_80050d34 and 176995 `jal FUN_8001f314` IN THE SAME TICK. FUN_8001f314.c:18 → FUN_8001f3bc.c:78 decrements +0x8f (frac 7→6) and FUN_8001f3bc.c:89-90 increments +0x95 (frame 0→1). So the original seeds AND advances on one tick. Our code psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/player_common.c:317-323 + include/re15_actor.h:187 inserts motion_init_delay=1 as a SEPARATE non-advancing hold tick (no frame++/frac--), deferring the advance to tick 2.
verdict.fix_summary: The audit's premise is wrong (the FSM IS disassembled at ghidra1_V2.txt:176956 LAB_80050cb8), but the underlying suspicion is RIGHT: our motion_init_delay over-preserves the frac=7 blend frame. The original's state-0 init tick seeds frac=7/frame=0 and then immediately calls the anim processor in the SAME tick (80050d30 falls through to 80050d34 jal FUN_8001f314), which advances frame 0->1 and decrements frac 7->6. Our code holds frame at 0 AND holds frac for one extra non-advancing tick (player_common.c:317-323), so every clip starts one 30Hz frame late and shows the frame0/frac7 pose one render-frame longer than the original. Byte-true fix: on the init tick, render frame 0 at frac=7 then advance in the SAME tick (frame++, frac--) instead of merely decrementing motion_init_delay; i.e. drop the separate hold tick (set motion_init_delay path to also do anim_frame++/anim_frac-- once, or remove motion_init_delay and let the normal advance run on the seed tick). Apply identically to the NPC loop (player_common.c:339-346) which mirrors the same hold. NOTE: re-verify against a DuckStation savestate before shipping since the one-frame magnitude is subtle.

claim: Our DOOR fires on a SINGLE in-reach action frame (door_inside && g_aot_action_pressed). The original requires a HOLD: an in-reach+action counter (obj-2) that increments each frame in reach, resets to 0 when out, and only triggers the door-open (FUN_8003b558(obj,2)) when the counter reaches 9. We open ~9 frames too early / without the debounce.
disasm_cite: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8002bd44.c:57-60 (type==4 door && action(DAT_800ac768&1) && band match && FUN_8002d1e8(...) -> counter obj[-2]++), :63 (else counter=0), :65 (counter=='\t'(9) -> FUN_8002cabc + FUN_8003b558 door open)
fix: Add a per-door in-reach hold counter in the AOT state: each frame DOOR is door_inside && action_pressed && band-match, increment a->reach_count (reset to 0 otherwise); only fire the door teleport when reach_count reaches 9 (FUN_8002bd44.c:65). Keep the single-action-per-frame latch.

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8002bd44.c:57-65 (held action DAT_800ac768&1 + forward-reach FUN_8002d1e8 -> counter puVar4[-2]++ at :60, else =0 at :63, fires FUN_8003b558(obj,2) only when counter=='\t'=9 at :65); held-vs-edge proven by FUN_80030444.c:11-28 (DAT_800ac768=held level rebuilt each frame; the edge is the separate DAT_800ac76c=:28). Our deviating code: psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/aot_common.c:370-371 fires on a single edge frame (door_inside && g_aot_action_pressed), and g_aot_action_pressed is the EDGE (game_step_common.c:27 = c->pad_pressed = re15_engine.h:35 "bits set this frame only" == original DAT_800ac76c, NOT the held DAT_800ac768).
verdict.fix_summary: Real deviation. Original requires a ~9-frame HELD debounce: while the player is in forward-reach of a type-4 door AND the action button is HELD (DAT_800ac768&1, the LEVEL mask) AND the band matches, a per-door counter obj[-2] increments each frame (FUN_8002bd44.c:60), resets to 0 the moment any of those fail (:63), and the door only opens (FUN_8003b558(obj,2)) once the counter reaches 9 (:65). Our code instead opens on the single press EDGE (g_aot_action_pressed = c->pad_pressed = newly-pressed bits) with no cross-frame counter — so we open ~9 frames early and without the hold requirement. Fix byte-true: (1) add a per-door int reach_count to the AOT/door state; (2) gate it on the HELD action level (expose a g_aot_action_held like g_scd_action_held = c->pad_current & SQUARE, mirroring DAT_800ac768) NOT the edge; (3) each frame, if door_inside (the 563-forward-reach test, = FUN_8002d1e8) && action_held && band-match, do reach_count++, else reach_count=0; (4) only fire the door teleport when reach_count==9. Keep the existing per-frame action_fired latch. Note the original uses the HELD mask, so the existing g_aot_action_pressed (edge) is the wrong input for the counter increment.

claim: The player-band == AOT-band gate is applied in our scan only to RE15_AOT_TYPE_DOOR (door_params[i].band). The original SCE scan FUN_80042bac applies the band compare to EVERY SCE entry (all types) unless the entry's byte[2] high bit is set; non-door MESSAGE/GENERIC/ITEM AOTs are therefore not band-gated in our code though the original gates them.
disasm_cite: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80042bac.c:59-61 ((pbVar9[2]&0x80)!=0 || *(param_1+0x82)==pbVar9[2]) — band compare in the generic SCE loop before the type dispatch, applies to all types; pbVar9[2] = SCD opcode pc[4] (op_aot_set ignores pc[4] for non-door/non-stair types, scd_vm.c:1756-1787)
fix: In op_aot_set, store pc[4] as the band byte for ALL AOT types (and a skip flag when pc[4]&0x80). In re15_aot_scan, gate every AOT (not just DOOR) on (band&0x80) || player_band==band, mirroring FUN_80042bac.c:59-61. Currently latent because the room1170/1130/1140 message/generic AOTs that were tuned happen not to exercise a mismatched band, but it is not byte-true.

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80042bac.c:59-61 — `if (((pbVar9[1] & 0x10) == param_3) && (((pbVar9[2] & 0x80) != 0 || (... *(byte *)(param_1 + 0x82) == pbVar9[2]))))` — the band compare (zone_band pc[4]==object_band, unless pc[4]&0x80) wraps the ENTIRE per-type dispatch (handler called via PTR_LAB_8007469c[*pbVar9] at FUN_80042bac.c:132/136/145/148), so it applies to ALL SCE/AOT types, not just doors. This is the per-object SCE-zone scan our re15_aot_scan ports (auto caller FUN_800436a8.c:8 passes the player object &DAT_800aca54; param_1+0x82 = that object's band). Our code at psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/aot_common.c:346-348 applies the band gate ONLY inside `if (a->type == RE15_AOT_TYPE_DOOR)`; no MESSAGE/GENERIC/ITEM/EXAMINE path band-gates, and op_aot_set (scd_vm.c:1756-1788) never stores pc[4] as a band for non-door/non-stair types. Real room data confirms non-door AOTs have pc[4]=0 (NOT 0x80=skip): extracted/PSX/STAGE1/room1170/scd/main00.c:26 `Aot_set(5, 1, 49, 0, ...)` (band-0 pit MESSAGE locked-door) and room1130/scd/main00.c:8 `Aot_set(4, 3, 49, 0, ...)` (GENERIC switch) — both band-gated to band 0 in the original. NOTE the cited door-band mechanism in our comment (aot_common.c:336, FUN_8002bd44.c:57-58) is a SEPARATE object-pool loop that gates only type-4 door objects; it does not cover the SCE message/generic AOTs that FUN_80042bac gates.
verdict.fix_summary: Real deviation (latent in the single-band rooms 1130/1140 but divergent in multi-band room1170). In op_aot_set, store pc[4] as a `band` byte for EVERY AOT type (plus a skip flag when pc[4]&0x80), mirroring the door/stair paths that already capture pc[4]. In re15_aot_scan, gate EVERY non-stair AOT (MESSAGE/GENERIC/ITEM/EXAMINE/AUTO_EVENT, not just DOOR) on `(band&0x80) || player_band==band` exactly as FUN_80042bac.c:59-61, using re15_collision_debug_band() and the established `pb>=0` guard. Concretely: room1170 slot-5 (Aot_set(5,1,...,pc4=0)) is the band-0 pit locked-door MESSAGE; with no gate, a band-4 courtyard player whose forward-reach overlaps that rect can fire "It's locked from the other side" on the wrong floor, which the original suppresses.

claim: The main resolver re15_collision_constrain (and its quadrant select / broad-phase / push handlers) drops the SCA section ORIGIN that the original adds to the player position before every cell comparison. The original FUN_8003b0a4 passes local_38 = *psVar13 (psVar13 = DAT_800ac784[0x1f]) as an (X,Z) offset and computes the broad-phase as ((originX + playerX) - (cellX - r)) and the push deltas relative to *(DAT_800ac784+0x7c)[0/2] (= the SAME psVar13). FUN_8003b068 likewise adds param_2 (origin) before the ceiling subtraction. Our code uses origin=0 at all three sites (documented 'origin = 0' in the header). NOTE the floor-query FUN_8003b7f0 genuinely zeroes the origin (auStack_18=0 at its :19) so our on_floor is correct there — but the RESOLVER does NOT. ROOM1170 is user-verified working, so the origin is effectively 0 for it, but the value cannot be proven statically (the runtime SCA struct at &DAT_800b2248 differs from the parsed collision.sca file; psVar13[0]/[2] would need a RAM dump). If nonzero, expose it as e.g. rdt->sca_origin_x/z and add it to the player X/Z inside quadrant_of, the broad-phase test (lines 298-299), push_rect (penX/penZ base) and push_circle (dx/dz base) — exactly as the disasm folds *(DAT_800ac784+0x7c)[0/2] into iVar2/iVar7. claim: Slope/diagonal collision types 2,4,5,6,7 are intentionally NOT handled (player passes through those cells). The dispatch table proves these map to real handlers (LAB_8003d00c, LAB_8003beb0, LAB_8003c734, LAB_8003cb9c, LAB_8003c2cc). This is a documented deferral, not present in ROOM1170, but it IS a behavioral gap for slope rooms.
fix: Decompile the five slope handlers and implement them (deferred per [[slope_collision_deferred_2026_06_09]]). Zero ROOM1170 impact; required before any room with slope SCA cells is considered byte-true.

Our code psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/re15_collision.c:300-308 handles only type 1/3/8/9 and explicitly defers 2/4/5/6/7. verdict.fix_summary: Real, citable byte-true deviation (could NOT refute it): the original resolver FUN_8003b0a4 dispatches SCA cell `type` (byte at entry+0x8) through the runtime-filled jump table at 0x800b2858 and calls real push-out handlers for types 2,4,5,6,7 (LAB_8003d00c/8003beb0/8003c734/8003cb9c/8003c2cc), whereas re15_collision.c:300-308 implements only 1/3/8/9 and lets the player pass through 2/4/5/6/7 cells. NOT a legit RE2/SDL/synth arch difference -- it is an unimplemented original behavior. HOWEVER it is a LATENT gap with provably zero impact on the only byte-true-verified room: I enumerated extracted/PSX/STAGE1/room1170/collision.sca (150 cells, header rgn 30x5) and every dispatch byte is type 1 (110) or type 3 (40) -- no slope/diagonal cells exist there. Fix = decompile and port the five handlers (LAB_8003d00c etc.) into the else-chain, but it is low priority: only required before any room with type 2/4/5/6/7 SCA cells is claimed byte-true. The prior 'uncertain' filing was over-conservative -- the dispatch mapping is now confirmed correct, so this should be reclassified 'deviation (latent)'.

claim: The object (prop) X/Z least-penetration push matches FUN_8002cabc: overlap gate via |dx|>sumX / |dz|>sumZ (the unsigned-wrap idiom), least-pen select via |dx*sumZ| <= |dz*sumX| -> push Z else push X, and the face-clamp signs (dz>=0 -> bz-sumZ, dx<0 -> bx+sumX) all match. The omitted Y-axis gate (the swept-Y branch lines 18-71) is a documented, user-confirmed simplification for the flat helipad (player vertical box not extracted).
disasm_cite: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8002cabc.c:28-33 (unsigned overlap gate), :72-79 (|iVar12*iVar8| vs |iVar11*iVar9| least-pen select), :84-90 (Z push sign), :100-105 (X push sign); Y-gate at :37-71
fix: none (Y-gate omission accepted; would need the player vertical collision box to port the swept-Y branch byte-true)

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8002cabc.c:39-40 (bVar1 = Y-overlap test `(uint)(iVar10+iVar4) <= (uint)(iVar4*2)`, then `if (bVar1) {`) gates the ENTIRE horizontal push (lines 40-108), plus the swept-Y vertical-resolution branch at :49-71 (writes param_1[0xe]=Y when Y-overlap state changes). Our re15_collision.c:340-350 has the X gate (:340) and Z gate (:341) but NO Y-overlap gate and NO swept-Y branch — the horizontal push at :343-350 runs unconditionally once X/Z overlap.
verdict.fix_summary: The audit's HORIZONTAL math (overlap gate :28-33, least-pen select :72-79, face-clamp signs :84-105) is genuinely byte-true — that part of the claim holds. But the audit mis-classifies the whole finding as `ok`: the original (FUN_8002cabc.c:40 `if(bVar1)`) only applies the horizontal push when the boxes ALSO overlap in Y (bVar1, computed :37-39 from psVar6[4]/psVar7[4] half-extents). Our port omits that Y-overlap gate entirely, so in any room with a prop whose Y extent does not span the player (overhead beam, high crate, low cleared step) we would push the player horizontally where the original lets him pass — a provable deviation, not an accepted RE2-arch difference (same PSX routine). It is NOT engine_specific; it is a ROOM1170-only simplification (flat helipad → every crate always overlaps Y → bVar1 always true → identical there only). pl->y IS available at the call site (game_step_common.c:75), and props already carry box_hy/box_cy (scd_vm.c:2476/2479), so a byte-true fix is feasible: pass pl->y + a player Y half-extent, compute dy = (box_cy+box_y) - (player_y+player_cy), gate the push on |dy| <= box_hy + player_PRY (mirroring :39), and ideally port the swept-Y branch (:49-71). The only genuinely unextracted constant is the player Y half-extent (psVar7[4]) — find it (likely DAT_80073e94 neighbor of PR=index6) before implementing. NOTE: our :330 all-zero `bhx&&bhy&&bhz==0 continue` early-out is an added optimization not in the disasm but behaviorally harmless (zero box can't overlap).

claim: The orbit cam-pos formula is explicitly SKIPPED ('cam_pos held at static cut.pos'), with a comment that the fixed-point trig round-trip 'loses ~14%'. The original computes cam.x/cam.z each frame from yaw+distance residual.
disasm_cite: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80015850.c:9-15: DAT_800b5234 = (rcos(DAT_800b525c)*DAT_800b5258 >> 0xc) + cut.target.x ; DAT_800b523c = (rsin(...)*DAT_800b5258 >> 0xc) + cut.target.z
fix: Implement the orbit: cam_pos[0] = ((re15_cos_q12(yaw_q12)*dist_residual)>>12) + cut_static.target_x ; cam_pos[2] = ((re15_sin_q12(yaw_q12)*dist_residual)>>12) + cut_static.target_z. Use the SAME rcos/rsin Q12 helpers (re15_cos_q12/re15_sin_q12) the engine uses (>>0xc) so it is byte-identical; the '14% loss' was a wrong-table artifact, not an inherent loss. With dist_step=yaw_step=0 (no dolly) this reproduces the static pos exactly while making future cinematic dolly sub-handlers work.

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80015850.c:9-15 (per-frame: DAT_800b5234=cam.x=(rcos(yaw)*dist>>0xc)+target.x ; DAT_800b523c=cam.z=(rsin(yaw)*dist>>0xc)+target.z ; yaw DAT_800b525c-=DAT_800b525e &0xfff). Init proves the orbit actually MOVES: ghidra1_V2.txt:81356 sets yaw_step DAT_800b525e=0x384 (900/frame, NONZERO) and ghidra1_V2.txt:81347-81360 sets dist_step DAT_800b5260=(dist-0xce4)/3 (nonzero). Init also writes the authored cam pos directly (ghidra1_V2.txt:81315-81333, lw 0x4/0xc/0x8 of cut struct) so frame1=authored, frame2+=orbit. Our code holds cam_pos at cut_static.pos forever (camera_common.c:370-372) with yaw_step=0/dist_step=0 (camera_common.c:335-338) — provably static where the original orbits.
verdict.fix_summary: REAL deviation: the original recomputes cam.x/cam.z every frame from rcos/rsin(yaw)*dist (FUN_80015850.c:9-15) and the orbit genuinely moves because init seeds yaw_step=0x384 and dist_step=(dist-0xce4)/3 (ghidra1_V2.txt:81356, :81347-81360) — NOT zero. Our tick skips the formula and holds the static cut pos, so the cinematic camera does not orbit/dolly during the cut. BUT the prior audit's proposed fix is wrong on a load-bearing point: it claims 'with yaw_step=dist_step=0 this reproduces the static pos exactly' — that would still be static (just trig-reconstructed) and is NOT byte-true. A byte-true fix must (1) port the formula cam_pos[0]=((cos(yaw)*dist)>>12)+target.x, cam_pos[2]=((sin(yaw)*dist)>>12)+target.z, AND (2) seed yaw_step=0x384 and dist_step=(dist-0xce4)/3 at init, AND (3) use a PSX-accurate ratan2/rcos/rsin round-trip — not our approximate 45-degree cam_atan2_q12 (camera_common.c:292-309), which is what actually caused the '~14% loss' the comment blames on the formula. The focus-track divisors /60 and /20 (camera_common.c:389-391) already match /0x3c,/0x14 (FUN_80015850.c:16-20) and are OK.

claim: The finalize tick performs an invented 48-step forward collision march that re-positions the player (p->x/p->z) along rot_y to the first walkable cell. The ORIGINAL finalize does NO X/Z reposition whatsoever — it only swaps to clip 2, resets the cursor, restores control, and recomputes the band from the committed Y. Our own comment (lines 127-139) explicitly admits 'The original's finalize does NOT reposition the player at all'. This is a heuristic compensating for our approximated forward+FK travel.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:136857-136893 (LAB_80038e50: loads DAT_800acad8/acbc0, sets DAT_800acae8=2 clip, DAT_800acae9=0 cursor, DAT_800acae3=0, jal FUN_8001f314, DAT_800aca59=0, DAT_800aca5a=0, DAT_800aca5b=0, DAT_800acae7&=0xfe, then band = -DAT_800acc0e/0x708 via the 0x91a2b3c5 multiply at 136871-136893 — NO writes to entity X(0x34)/Z(0x3c))

Drop the 48-step march entirely. If the travel cannot be made byte-true, this remains a documented hack, not byte-true.

The only XZ-touching calls in the per-band body LAB_80038c60 are FUN_800390e0 (FK foot-lock, jal @ghidra1_V2.txt:136789) and FUN_800245d8 (forward, jal @136820); the finalize's own jal FUN_8001f314 is the anim/FK cursor-advance helper (FUN_8001f314 body @ghidra1_V2.txt:98294-98339 only reads clip/cursor 0x94/0x95 and writes the keyframe ptr 0x168, no entity X/Z write). Our deviation: psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/stair_common.c:140-168 runs a 48-iteration re15_collision_constrain march writing p->x/p->z, with the code's own admission at stair_common.c:127-139 that 'The original finalize does NOT reposition the player at all'.
verdict.fix_summary: Confirmed real byte-true deviation; could not refute. The cite is accurate: LAB_80038e50 (136857-136893) only swaps to clip 2, zeroes the cursor/flags, restores control (DAT_800aca59/5a/5b), masks DAT_800acae7&=0xfe, and recomputes band = -DAT_800acc0e/0x708 — it makes zero writes to entity X(0x34)/Z(0x3c). The jal'd FUN_8001f314 (98294) is the anim cursor/FK helper, not a reposition, and the per-band XZ in LAB_80038c60 comes solely from FUN_800390e0 (FK) + FUN_800245d8 (forward). Our stair_common.c:140-168 48-step forward-eject march is invented and admitted as a heuristic at lines 127-139. Byte-true fix: in the finalize tick do ONLY motion=clip2 (IDLE), anim_frame=0, anim_flags=0, restore control, p->y=s_target_y, band=re15_collision_band_from_y(p->y) — drop the entire for(step<48) march; rely on the next gameplay frame's FUN_8003b0a4 floor resolver (the same mechanism the original depends on) to eject. This requires the per-band forward+FK travel to actually overshoot the destination wall-cell midpoint as the original's does; if our approximated travel cannot reach that landing reliably, the march remains a documented hack rather than byte-true and the real forward/FK constants (FUN_800245d8 speed, FUN_800390e0 foot-drag) must be corrected so the landing matches.

claim: Heuristic '+100u forward nudge then re-test' to force a forward edge. No such loop or constant exists in the original — the original never repositions in finalize, so there is nothing to nudge.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:136857-136893 (LAB_80038e50 contains no per-axis nudge / collision-constrain loop; only the band division at 136871-136893)
fix: Remove together with finding #1 (the whole eject march). The 100u constant has no disasm basis.

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:136857-136893 (LAB_80038e50, descend finalize): the ONLY operations are DAT_800acae8=2 (standby clip, 136864), DAT_800acae9=0 cursor reset (136866), DAT_800acae3=0 (136868), jal FUN_8001f314 (136869), the band recompute DAT_800acad6 = -DAT_800acc0e/0x708 via the 0x91a2b3c5 reciprocal multiply (136871-136893), and clearing DAT_800aca59/5a/5b + DAT_800acae7&=0xfe (136880-136886). No X/Z store, no per-axis nudge, no collision-constrain/eject loop, no 100-constant. The ascend finalize at 136680-136710 is identical in shape (same band division, no reposition), corroborating. Our stair_common.c:147-161 invents an eject march and stair_common.c:159-160 advances the probe by `(cs*100)>>12` / `(-sn*100)>>12` — the 100u nudge — which has no counterpart in either finalize handler. Our own comment (stair_common.c:132-139) admits it is an autopilot-derived APPROXIMATION workaround, not a disasm-cited value.
verdict.fix_summary: Remove the entire fabricated forward-eject march (stair_common.c:140-169), including the +100u nudge at lines 159-160. Byte-true LAB_80038e50 does NOT reposition the player in finalize: it only commits Y (p->y = s_target_y, already done at 125), recomputes the collision band from the committed Y (band = -DAT_800acc0e/0x708, our re15_collision_set_band(re15_collision_band_from_y(p->y)) at 126 matches), switches to standby clip 2 / restores control, and hands back. The original relies on the NEXT gameplay frame's floor resolver (FUN_8003b0a4) to un-stick the player from the destination wall cell — which works because on that frame old==new (stationary landing). This finding should be removed together with finding #1 (the whole eject loop), not patched in isolation. The 100 constant is an uncited heuristic and must go.

claim: Entry accepts a stair zone only when zone.band == player_band (single band, margin 450). The ORIGINAL entry FUN_8002d474 runs TWO scan passes testing FOUR band relations against zone[0x82]: loop1 tests player_band+1 and player_band-2 with margin 0xc8=200; loop2 tests player_band-1 and player_band with margin 0x1c2=450. We are missing the +1 / -2 / -1 band relations and the 200-margin pass entirely, so we only trigger one of the original's four cases.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:120491-120503 (loop1: a3=DAT_800acad6+1 then a3=DAT_800acad6-2, a2=0xc8) and ghidra1_V2.txt:120565-120578 (loop2: a3=DAT_800acad6-1 then a3=DAT_800acad6 unchanged, a2=0x1c2); band compare in FUN_8002da4c ghidra1_V2.txt:120868-120870 (lbu v0,0x82(slot); bne v0,a3 -> reject)
fix: Replace the single `band==cur` test with the four byte-true relations: pass A (margin 200) accept zone[0x82] in {cur+1, cur-2}; pass B (margin 450) accept zone[0x82] in {cur-1, cur}. Use FUN_8002da4c's exact rect+margin test (margin added to BOTH x and z extents).

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:120868-120870 (FUN_8002da4c: lbu v0,0x82(t0); bne v0,a3,reject => band byte 0x82 compared to PASSED-IN band a3, not a fixed value; then rect+margin test adds a2/t1 to both extents at 120885 & 120890). Caller FUN_8002d474 issues FOUR tests per AOT: loop1 ghidra1_V2.txt:120491-120503 (a3=DAT_800acad6+1, a2=0xc8=200; then a3=DAT_800acad6-2, a2=0xc8); loop2 ghidra1_V2.txt:120565-120579 (a3=DAT_800acad6-1, a2=0x1c2=450; then a3=DAT_800acad6 unchanged, a2=0x1c2). DAT_800acad6 = player band (set at ghidra1_V2.txt:136010/136196/136272 by the band updater; read as 'cur'). FUN_8002d474 is the 800u-forward player-action AOT scan (probe DAT_800109c8=0x320=800 at 120421, same forward probe our code uses). Our code psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/stair_common.c:300-302 tests only `(int)a->band == cur`, i.e. exactly ONE of the four original cases (cur, margin 450). The cite is byte-accurate and the three other relations (cur+1@200, cur-2@200, cur-1@450) plus the ordered two-pass tie-break are absent.
verdict.fix_summary: Real deviation; could not refute. Replace the single `(int)a->band == cur` test in re15_stair_try_start with the byte-true four-relation, two-pass scan of FUN_8002d474: Pass A (margin 200) accept zone[0x82] in {cur+1, cur-2}; Pass B (margin 450) accept zone[0x82] in {cur-1, cur}, evaluated in that order (first match wins). Use FUN_8002da4c's exact rect test with the margin added to BOTH x and z extents (point_in_zone currently uses a fixed margin; it must take the per-pass margin 200/450). Note our current `band==cur` is the (cur,450) Pass-B-second case only, so stairs whose AOT band byte is cur-1/cur+1/cur-2 relative to the standing band are wrongly rejected. Caveat: the band-gate was originally added (memory 2026-06-08) to stop the top stair firing at the door below; the byte-true fix preserves disambiguation via the ordered passes + tolerance window rather than strict equality, so re-validate the room1170 top-stair-vs-door case after applying.

claim: Only the 450 (0x1c2) margin is implemented; the original's first AOT pass uses margin 0xc8=200. A stair reachable only by the 200-margin pass will be missed (or one reachable only by 450 wrongly accepted at the wrong band).
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:120492 (ori a2,zero,0xc8 = 200 for loop1) vs ghidra1_V2.txt:120566/120579 (ori a2,zero,0x1c2 = 450 for loop2)
fix: Parameterise the margin per pass: 200 for the {cur+1,cur-2} pass, 450 for the {cur-1,cur} pass, matching FUN_8002d474.

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:120492 (8002d5b4 `ori a2,zero,0xc8` = margin 200) and 120500 (8002d5d4, same 0xc8) are the AOT in-rect tests of FUN_8002da4c in Pass A; ghidra1_V2.txt:120566 (8002d698 `ori a2,zero,0x1c2` = margin 450) and 120579 (8002d6cc, same 0x1c2) are Pass B. FUN_8002da4c (ghidra1_V2.txt:120867-120900) decoded: a2/t1 is added to BOTH rect half-width (8002da94 `addu v0,v0,t1`) and half-height (8002daa8 `addu a2,v1,t1`) — i.e. a2 is the rect-expansion MARGIN, confirming 0xc8=200 / 0x1c2=450 are margins, exactly as the claim states. Band args per pass: Pass A a3=band+1 (8002d5b8 `addiu a3,a3,0x1`) and band-2 (8002d5d8 `addiu a3,a3,-0x2`); Pass B a3=band-1 (8002d69c `addiu a3,a3,-0x1`) and band+0 (8002d6cc unmodified DAT_800acad6). Our stair_common.c:301 gates `(int)a->band == cur` (= band+0 only) and our two zone tests use margin 0 (point_in_zone, stair_common.c:81-84) and 450 (player_in_zone, stair_common.c:77-78 via STAIR_REACH=450 at :39) — the 200-margin scan and the band+1/band-1/band-2 reach are entirely absent.
verdict.fix_summary: Confirmed deviation — could not refute. FUN_8002d474 runs a TWO-margin, MULTI-BAND AOT stair scan; our entry replicates only one band (cur) at margin 450 (plus a non-original margin-0 probe). Byte-true fix: replace the single `a->band==cur` + (0|450) test with the original's two passes against FUN_8002da4c's expanded-rect semantics (margin added to both half-extents): Pass with margin 0xc8=200 over bands {cur+1, cur-2}, and pass with margin 0x1c2=450 over bands {cur-1, cur}. The claim's band->margin mapping ({cur+1,cur-2}=200, {cur-1,cur}=450) is exactly correct per the disasm. Caveat (claim understates scope, does not refute it): the two passes are MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE, selected by `s5=FUN_8002d2c0(probe)` (ghidra1_V2.txt:120459-120463: `bne s5,zero`->Pass A, else `andi v0,s0,0x1; beq`->Pass B); FUN_8002d2c0 (120255+) returns nonzero when the probe is in a 0x4000-latched zone or an active zone matching player-band with margin -0x70. A fully byte-exact port should reproduce that selector rather than running both passes unconditionally. Note also the 200-margin Pass-A band offsets (cur+1/cur-2) conflict with the existing 2026-06-08 "TOP-stair-at-door" guard comment at stair_common.c:289-299, so re-validate that bug does not regress when widening the band reach.

loc: psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/stair_common.c:196-233 (FK foot-lock: s_footref = previous-tick cached foot world pos)
claim: Our foot-lock subtracts the foot's PER-TICK world delta using a cache (s_footref) of last tick's computed foot world pos. The original FUN_800390e0 (a0==0 branch) subtracts (currentFootWorld - s3[0x54/58/5c]) where s3[0x54..] is a FIXED bone field in the pose/work struct (entity[0x188]+bone stride), read each call and NEVER written by this function — i.e. it is the bone's stored reference translation, not a previous-tick snapshot. Modeling it as a per-tick delta may accumulate differently across the band loop (we also zero s_footref_ok at each band loop, line 249).
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:137100-137126 (LAB after bne s2,zero: p[0x34]-= local_24-s3[0x54]; p[0x3c]-= local_1c-s3[0x5c]; p[0x38]-= local_20-s3[0x58]); s3 = entity[0x188] + (footsel*0x204 + 0x2b0) per 137083-137087; no sw to 0x54/0x58/0x5c of s3 anywhere (grep: only reads at 137105/137113/137121/137129/137136)
fix: Determine what entity[0x188]+0x54 (per bone) actually holds (likely the previous render pass's accumulated bone translation written by the FK pose builder FUN_8001f3bc/FUN_8001f664, not by FUN_800390e0). Source footPrev from that exact field/pass so the body-drag matches byte-for-byte instead of a local cache reset per band.

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:137105 (lw a0,0x54(s3)) + 137107-137109 (subu v1,local_24,a0; subu v0,body0x34,v1; sw body0x34) prove body -= (curFootWorld - s3[0x54]); s3 = entity[0x188]+footsel*0x204+0x2b0 per 137083-137087. s3[0x54/0x58/0x5c] is READ at 137105/137113/137121 (and a0!=0 branch 137129/137136) but NEVER written by FUN_800390e0. Exhaustive grep for a per-tick vector-triple store to 0x54/0x58/0x5c finds only UNRELATED structs (globals DAT_800a749x at 86593-86599/87181-87187; TILE DAT_800b52d8 at 121456-121493) — no per-tick snapshot of the locked foot is ever written back. The SAME pattern is the engine's standard per-frame walk foot-lock FUN_800369f8 (ghidra1_V2.txt:133000-133035, identical body -= local_24-s3[0x54]). => s3[0x54..] is the pose's per-frame STORED reference foot (written by the FK builder rebuilding entity[0x188]), an ABSOLUTE reference re-read each frame, NOT a previous-tick snapshot. Our stair_common.c:225-232 instead subtracts a PER-TICK delta of the LOCKED foot vs a local cache s_footref, reset per band at line 249 — structurally different (telescopes to curFoot_now - curFoot_at_seed vs original's curFoot_now - reference_now).
verdict.fix_summary: Source footPrev from the pose's per-frame reference (the bone field the FK pose-builder writes into entity[0x188]+footsel*0x204+0x2b0+0x54/58/5c), not from a per-tick cache. Concretely: each tick compute BOTH the current locked-foot world (local_24/20/1c equiv = wx/wy/wz) AND the same-frame REFERENCE foot world (the un-locked/bind foot from the pose builder, the equivalent of s3[0x54..]), and do body -= (curFoot - reference) every tick with no s_footref carry and no per-band reset. The reference must be re-derived each frame from the pose (matching FUN_8001f3bc/FUN_8001f664 writing the 0x204-stride per-foot block), so the body-drag is an absolute per-frame correction byte-for-byte, not an accumulating per-tick delta. Until the exact 0x204-block writer is pinned, treat this as a real modeling deviation to fix, not a tunable.

claim: The Member_cmp comparison-operator mapping is wrong. Our switch maps {0:==, 1:!=, 2:<, 3:>, default:false}. The original maps 7 ops {0:==, 1:>, 2:>=, 3:<, 4:<=, 5:!=, 6:bittest}. So our op 1/2/3 produce the wrong relation and ops 4/5/6 wrongly fall to false.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:151444-151468 (LAB_80041290 switchD_800412f0: caseD_0 xor;sltiu=eq; caseD_1 slt s1,v1=cur>val; caseD_2 slt v1,s1;xori1=cur>=val; caseD_3 slt v1,s1=cur<val; caseD_4 slt s1,v1;xori1=cur<=val; caseD_5 xor;sltu=ne; caseD_6 and;sltu=bittest)
fix: Replace the switch with: case0:cur==arg; case1:cur>arg; case2:cur>=arg; case3:cur<arg; case4:cur<=arg; case5:cur!=arg; case6:(cur&arg)!=0; default:false (sltiu v0,a2,0x7 caps at 7).

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:151444-151468 (switchD_800412f0): case0 xor;sltiu=EQ; case1 `slt v0,s1,v1`=cur>arg(GT); case2 `slt v0,v1,s1;xori 1`=cur>=arg(GE); case3 `slt v0,v1,s1`=cur<arg(LT); case4 `slt v0,s1,v1;xori 1`=cur<=arg(LE); case5 xor;sltu=NE; case6 and;sltu=bittest. Register binding at ghidra1_V2.txt:151424-151431: s1=lh[v0+4]=arg, v1=v0=member current value(cur), cmp_op=s0>>8 capped by `sltiu v0,s0,0x7` (151432). Our code at psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/scd_vm.c:2139-2145 maps {0:==,1:!=,2:<,3:>,default:false} — op1/2/3 give the wrong relation and op4/5/6 wrongly fall to false.
verdict.fix_summary: Replace the 4-case switch with all 7 ops matching the disasm: case0 cur==arg; case1 cur>arg; case2 cur>=arg; case3 cur<arg; case4 cur<=arg; case5 cur!=arg; case6 (cur&arg)!=0; default 0 (the `sltiu v0,s0,0x7` already caps the index at 7→default). The arg endianness (BE) is untouched by this fix and is a separate, out-of-scope question. Note the sibling op_cmp (0x3D) at scd_vm.c:2110-2119 already uses the correct 7-op table, confirming op_member_cmp's 4-op table is an incomplete port.

claim: Member_cmp reads its compare value BIG-ENDIAN via scd_read_be_s16(&t->pc[4]); the inline comment claims it is 'genuinely mixed-endian'. The original reads it little-endian (R3000 `lh`).
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:151424 (LAB_80041290: `800412ac 04 00 51 84  lh s1,0x4(v0)` — lh is little-endian on the LE R3000; PC then +=6 at 800412b0)
fix: Read the value LE: int16_t arg = scd_read_le_s16(&t->pc[4]); and drop the 'stays BIG-ENDIAN' comment. (member_id=pc[2], cmp_op=pc[3] are already correct: lhu@0x2 -> andi 0xff = pc[2], sra 8 = pc[3].)

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:151424 (`800412ac 04 00 51 84  lh s1,0x4(v0)` inside LAB_80041290, the Member_cmp handler, XREF 800745a0). On the LE PSX R3000, `lh` loads the halfword in little-endian byte order (p[0] | p[1]<<8). Surrounding instructions confirm this is exactly op_member_cmp's layout: ghidra1_V2.txt:151423 `lhu s0,0x2(v0)` then :151429 `andi a1,s0,0xff` (=member_id=pc[2]) and :151431 `sra s0,s0,0x8` (=cmp_op=pc[3]); :151425 `addiu v0,v0,0x6` (=pc+=6). Our code at scd_vm.c:2132 reads the +4 value via scd_read_be_s16 (defined scd_vm.c:530-533 as p[0]<<8 | p[1] = byte-SWAPPED), the opposite of `lh`.
verdict.fix_summary: At scd_vm.c:2132 change `int16_t arg = scd_read_be_s16(&t->pc[4]);` to `int16_t arg = scd_read_le_s16(&t->pc[4]);` (matches the `lh` at 800412ac). Drop the 'value path stays BIG-ENDIAN / genuinely mixed-endian' comment at scd_vm.c:2148-2151 and the 0x3E claim in the scd_read_be_s16 retention note (scd_vm.c:527-528). member_id=pc[2] and cmp_op=pc[3] are already byte-true (lhu@0x2 + andi 0xff / sra 8) and need no change.

claim: The Cmp (0x23) comparison-operator mapping is wrong for ops 2/3/4. Our switch maps {0:==,1:>,2:<,3:<=,4:>=,5:!=,6:&}. The original maps {0:==,1:>,2:>=,3:<,4:<=,5:!=,6:&}. Ops 2,3,4 are permuted.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:150042-150066 (LAB_8003ff68 switchD_8003ffc0, a0=var a1=val: caseD_0 ==; caseD_1 slt a1,a0=var>val; caseD_2 slt a0,a1;xori1=var>=val; caseD_3 slt a0,a1=var<val; caseD_4 slt a1,a0;xori1=var<=val; caseD_5 xor;sltu=!=; caseD_6 and;sltu=bittest)
fix: Swap the case bodies to: case2: v>=imm; case3: v<imm; case4: v<=imm (cases 0,1,5,6 already correct). Member_cmp 0x3E uses the identical operator table, so apply the same canonical mapping to both.

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:150042-150066 (Cmp 0x23 = LAB_8003ff68, dispatch slot 0x80074534 = base 0x800744a8 +0x23*4). With a0=work-var DAT_800b0fd0[pc[2]&0xff] (lh @150030) and a1=imm (lh pc[4] @150020): case0 xor;sltiu1 ==> ==; case1 slt a1,a0 ==> var>imm; case2 slt a0,a1;xori1 (150050-150052) ==> var>=imm; case3 slt a0,a1 (150055) ==> var<imm; case4 slt a1,a0;xori1 (150057-150059) ==> var<=imm; case5 xor;sltu ==> !=; case6 and;sltu ==> bittest. Original table = {0:==,1:>,2:>=,3:<,4:<=,5:!=,6:&}. Our scd_vm.c:2113-2115 = {2:v<imm,3:v<=imm,4:v>=imm} => cases 2/3/4 permuted/wrong. Member_cmp 0x3E (LAB_80041290, ghidra1_V2.txt:151444-151468) has the byte-identical switch (case2 slt v1,s1;xori1 ==> member>=imm, etc.), confirming both opcodes share the same 7-op table; our op_member_cmp (scd_vm.c:2139-2145) instead uses a wrong 4-op {0:==,1:!=,2:<,3:>} table.
verdict.fix_summary: Confirmed deviation; could not refute. In op_cmp (scd_vm.c:2113-2115) set case2: v>=imm; case3: v<imm; case4: v<=imm (cases 0,1,5,6 already byte-true). The disasm proves the original RE1.5 op table is {0:==,1:>,2:>=,3:<,4:<=,5:!=,6:&}, so cases 2/3/4 are permuted in our code. Additionally, op_member_cmp (0x3E, scd_vm.c:2139-2145) shares the IDENTICAL 7-op table in the original (LAB_80041290) but our code uses a wrong 4-op {0:==,1:!=,2:<,3:>} mapping — replace it with the same canonical 7-op table (==,>,>=,<,<=,!=,&) keyed on cmp_op, applied to (member_value vs imm). Caveat: scd_vm.c:2105 reads the Cmp var from pc[1], but the engine reads it from pc[2] (lhu @0x8003ff70, var=low byte) — a separate, already-documented latent issue (var index) that is independent of the operator-mapping fix and was intentionally left due to a PSX-boot regression; the operator-table fix here does not depend on resolving it.

claim: Cmp reads its work-var index from pc[1]; the original reads it from pc[2] (pc[1] is a separate selector byte). Our own comment acknowledges this and ships pc[1] anyway (a prior pc[2] trial was reverted because it crashed the PSX boot). Latent for the only tested gate (ROOM1150 sub01 where pc[1]==pc[2]==0).
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:150019,150023-150024,150030 (LAB_8003ff68: `lhu v1,0x2(v0)` then `andi v0,v1,0xff` = low byte = pc[2]; `sll v0,v0,1; lh a0, DAT_800b0fd0(v0)` indexes work_vars by pc[2])
fix: Read var_idx = t->pc[2] (and treat pc[1] as the targetType/array selector). Requires a PSX-verified boot before landing, per the existing in-code warning — the boot crash on the prior trial points to a downstream/array-selector interaction that must be RE'd first.

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:150019 (`lhu v1,0x2(v0)` loads the halfword at PC+0x2), :150023 (`andi v0,v1,0xff` = low byte = pc[2] → var index), :150024 (`sll v0,v0,1` → ×2 s16 index), :150025 (`srl a2,v1,0x8` = high byte = pc[3] = operator), :150020 (`lh a1,0x4(v0)` = pc[4..5] immediate), :150030 (`lh a0,0x0(at)=>DAT_800b0fd0` indexes work_vars by pc[2]). Our handler scd_vm.c:2105 reads `var_idx = t->pc[1]` — wrong source. Dispatcher scd_vm.c:479 (`uint8_t op = *t->pc`) confirms t->pc points at pc[0] (opcode) inside the handler, so the original's PC+0x2 corresponds exactly to our pc[2]. Internally our own op=pc[3] (line 2106) and imm=pc[4..5] (line 2107) already match the disasm's PC+0x3/PC+0x4, proving the layout is opcode/selector(pc1)/var(pc2)/op(pc3)/imm(pc4-5) and that pc[1] is the wrong var source.
verdict.fix_summary: Change scd_vm.c:2105 from `uint8_t var_idx = t->pc[1];` to `t->pc[2]` so the work-var index reads from pc[2] (DAT_800b0fd0[pc[2]]), matching LAB_8003ff68. Operator (pc[3]) and immediate (pc[4..5]) are already byte-true and need no change. CAVEAT per the in-code warning: a prior pc[2] trial crashed the PSX boot (psxspu timeout → PC=0) — pc[1] is evidently a separate selector/targetType byte whose semantics must be RE'd, because some boot-path Cmp must currently rely (latently) on the pc[1] read producing work_vars[0]. The byte-true var-index fix should land only behind a PSX-verified boot once that pc[1]-selector downstream interaction is understood. The deviation itself is real and confirmed; it is latent only because the one tested gate (ROOM1150 sub01) has pc[1]==pc[2]==0.

claim: Our Se_on field naming (bank=pc[1], sample_id=pc[2], volume=pc[3], pan=pc[4], raw_w0=pc[5]) does not match the original field structure: the original reads pc[1] as one byte (a3), pc[2..3] as a halfword (the id passed to the emitter), and pc[4..5] as a halfword whose low byte (pc[4]) is the 0..5 bank-type SWITCH selector.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:151744-151747 (LAB_80041624: `lh a1,0x4(s0)` then `andi a2,a1,0xff` (=pc[4]) `sltiu v0,a2,0x6` switch; `lbu a3,0x1(s0)`=pc[1]; `lh a0,0x2(s0)`=pc[2..3])
fix: If the downstream re15_se_resolve consumer relies on these field names matching disasm semantics, re-map: the bank-type selector is pc[4] not pc[1], and the emitter id is the pc[2..3] halfword. The positional LE coords (pc[6/8/10]) and PC+=12 are byte-true (lhu@6/8/0xa; addiu s0,0xc). Memory notes the SE path was reworked + user-verified, so flagging rather than asserting a fix.

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:151744-151750 (FUN_80041624 = 0x36 Se_on handler, jump-table slot 80074580 = base 800744a8 + 0x36*4): `lh a1,0x4(s0)` / `lbu a3,0x1(s0)` / `lh a0,0x2(s0)` / `andi a2,a1,0xff` (=pc[4]) / `sltiu v0,a2,0x6` proves the 0..5 bank-type SWITCH selector is pc[4], the emitter id is the pc[2..3] halfword (a0), and pc[1] is a separate byte. Packing at 151808-151815 folds pc[1]<<24 | pc[2]<<16 | pc[3] into arg0 of FUN_80045024; pos LE at 151797/151801/151805; PC+=12 at 151817-151818. Our op_se_on (scd_vm.c:1144-1148) instead labels pc[1]=bank, pc[2]=sample_id, pc[4]=pan; and play_se_on (audio_psx.c:337) GATES playback on `pc[1]!=2`, i.e. reads the selector from the wrong operand byte (disasm selector = pc[4]), and indexes the EDT with pc[2] alone rather than the pc[2..3] halfword.
verdict.fix_summary: Re-map op_se_on to the disasm field layout: selector = pc[4] (the andi/sltiu 0..5 switch), emitter id = the pc[2..3] LE halfword, pc[1] = a separate byte (the packed arg0 high byte). The pos_x/y/z LE reads (pc[6/8/10]) and PC+=12 are already byte-true. Crucially, the downstream play_se_on gate `bank != 2` (audio_psx.c:337) is keyed on pc[1] but the original switches on pc[4]; it should key off the pc[4] selector (or fully port FUN_80041624's 0..5 case→bank-base resolution). Whether this is audibly observable depends on whether pc[1]==intended-selector in the executed SCD streams, but the field POSITIONS provably differ from the disasm, so the parser mapping is a real deviation that should be corrected and the gate re-pointed to pc[4].

claim: On room re-entry our code memsets g_scd to 0, leaving the SCD global work-variable bank work_vars[0..3] = 0. The original room-reload chain re-initializes work_vars[0..3] to 0xFFFF (= -1), not 0. work_vars[] aliases DAT_800b0fd0 (op_switch reads DAT_800b0fd0[var] per scd_vm.c:1088). The reload's FUN_8003ebf4 (reached via FUN_8003ef6c->FUN_8003f0a0->FUN_8003ebf4) explicitly writes DAT_800b0fd0=0xffff, DAT_800b0fd2=0xffff, DAT_800b0fd4=0xffff, DAT_800b0fd6=0xffff (work-vars 0/1/2/3) plus DAT_800b102c=0. Our re-entry never sets the -1 sentinels. Functionally neutral for the ROOM1150 'work_vars[0]==2' examine check (0 and -1 both fail), but any sub00/sub01 Switch(var0..3) with a 'case 0' or 'default' would diverge between our 0 and the original's -1. The comment 'memset above zeroed it = default scenario 0 at boot' is wrong about vars 0-3.
disasm_cite: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8003ebf4.c:4-8 (DAT_800b0fd0/2/4/6 = 0xffff; DAT_800b102c = 0); call path RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8003ef6c.c:18 -> FUN_8003f0a0.c:28
fix: After the memset and before scd_vm_tick(), set g_scd.work_vars[0]=g_scd.work_vars[1]=g_scd.work_vars[2]=g_scd.work_vars[3]=(int16_t)0xFFFF (=-1) to mirror FUN_8003ebf4. (Same fix belongs in scd_vm_init for the boot path, since FUN_8003ebf4 runs on every room load including boot.) Leave work_vars[10] (the scenario, = DAT_800b0fe4 at offset 0x14) untouched — the reload preserves it, and our re-stamp at line 154 reproduces that.

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8003ebf4.c:4-8 (DAT_800b0fd0/2/4/6 = 0xffff = work_vars[0..3]=-1; DAT_800b102c=0); reachable on EVERY room load: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_800396fc.c:67 (room-load) -> FUN_8003ef6c.c:5 calls FUN_8003ebf4 directly, and FUN_8003ef6c.c:18 -> FUN_8003f0a0.c:28 calls it AGAIN after the thread tick. Aliasing verified: op_switch reads g_scd.work_vars[var_index] (scd_vm.c:1096), work_vars is int16_t[256] (re15_scd.h:317) so var0..3 == byte offsets 0/2/4/6 == DAT_800b0fd0/2/4/6, and var10 == 0x14 == DAT_800b0fe4 (the scenario, preserved at FUN_800396fc.c:53).
