verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:112870-112911 (switchD_800282b4::caseD_4): 0x80028340 `bne v0,zero,LAB_800283b0` branches PAST the scan when arg!=0; the arg==0 fall-through at 0x80028348-0x800283a8 runs the forward SCAN loop LAB_8002835c (slti v1,0x5 / v1,0x7 / v1,0xc / v1,0x9 = codes 5,6,9,A,B get an extra addiu s0,s0,1) repeating `bne v0,a0(=4)` until the NEXT 0x04 is found, then LAB_800283a0 loads THAT 0x04's arg and stores it (sllv <<s1) to DAT_800b8524. Decompile mirror: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80028134.c:69-88 (case 4: `if(uVar4==0){ while(bVar1!=4){ if((4<bVar1)&&((bVar1<7||((bVar1<0xc&&(8<bVar1)))))) pbVar7++; pbVar7++; bVar1=*pbVar7; } pbVar6=pbVar7+1; uVar4=*pbVar6; } DAT_800b8524 = uVar4<<bVar2`). DAT_800b8524 == our message_scroll (init 2<<bVar2 line20; fast-fwd gate `<4` line29 == msg_common.c:370 `message_scroll<4`; reloads typing timer lines140/189). Our msg_common.c:392 `if(b==0x04){ a=raw[parse+1]:2; if(a) message_scroll=a; parse+=2; continue; }` — on a==0 it leaves scroll unchanged and skips 2 bytes, NO forward scan. Provable divergence.
verdict.fix_summary: Implement the arg==0 forward scan at msg_common.c:392. When raw[parse+1]==0, start at p=parse+2 and walk control codes (codes 5,6,9,0xA,0xB are 2-byte: skip an extra byte; all others 1-byte) until raw[p]==0x04 is reached, then take raw[p+1] as the new message_scroll and set parse=p+2 (just past that 0x04's arg). When arg!=0 keep current behavior. NOTE: the original also applies `<<bVar2` (full-width shift, bVar2=(DAT_800b5456==0)) to the stored scroll in BOTH the arg!=0 and scanned cases — our code stores the raw arg with no shift; that shift is a separate latent item but should be cross-checked when fixing this handler. The scan path is rare in shipped .msg but byte-true divergent.

claim: The dialog-step TYPING state handles only 0x01,0x02,0x03,0x04,0x05,0x08 and treats everything else (including 0x06 call-substring, 0x07 return, 0x09/0x0A/0x0B extended) as a printable glyph (default parse++). The original consumes 0x06 (call FUN_80028840 substring), 0x07 (return to saved ptr+2) and 0x09/0x0A/0x0B (2-byte skip).
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:112920-112944 (caseD_6 FUN_80028840 substring, caseD_7 DAT_800b8530+2, caseD_a/b/9 → switchD_caseD_9: pbVar7++ then LAB_80028434 pbVar7++ = 2-byte skip) = FUN_80028134.c:93-110
fix: Add to the TYPING switch: 0x09/0x0A/0x0B → parse+=2 (skip arg, no reveal); 0x06 → enter the substring (or at minimum parse+=2 to not misrender it as a glyph); 0x07 → return. room1170-class .msg don't use 0x06/0x07 substrings, but 0x09-0x0B would currently render as glyphs.

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80028134.c:93-110 (the dialog FSM inner control-byte switch: case 6=substring FUN_80028840, case 7=return to DAT_800b8530+2, case 9/10/0xb=goto switchD_800282b4_caseD_9) + FUN_80028134.c:185-188 (caseD_9: pbVar7++ then LAB_80028434 pbVar7++ = 2-byte skip), corroborated 1:1 in ghidra1_V2.txt:112920-112944 (caseD_6 sw s0/lbu 0x1(s0)/jal FUN_80028840; caseD_7 lw DAT_800b8530 / addiu s0,v0,0x2; caseD_a/b/9 j LAB_80028434 _addiu s0,s0,0x1 then LAB_80028434 addiu s0,s0,0x1). Our code msg_common.c:373-397 TYPING loop handles only 0x01,0x02,0x03,0x04,0x05,0x08; line 394 default `g_scd.message_parse++` reveals any other byte as a glyph. The original gate at FUN_80028134.c:42 / ghidra1_V2.txt:112816-112819 ((byte)(*pbVar7-0xc)<0xec) proves bytes 0x01..0x0B are control bytes, so 0x09/0x0A/0x0B are NOT glyphs.
verdict.fix_summary: In the TYPING loop (msg_common.c case 0, before the glyph-print fallthrough at line 394) add: 0x09/0x0A/0x0B -> message_parse += 2 (consume control byte + 1 arg, no glyph reveal), matching caseD_9's two pbVar7++ (FUN_80028134.c:185-188). Add 0x06 -> enter the substring referenced by raw[parse+1] (or at minimum parse += 2 so it is not misrendered as a glyph; FUN_80028840 substring, lines 93-100) and 0x07 -> return to the saved pointer+2 (line 101-103). Note: room1170-class .msg do not exercise 0x06/0x07 substrings, but 0x09/0x0A/0x0B currently render as garbage glyphs. NOTE the original FSM-state numbers differ from our enum (orig 0x01->state5/6, 0x02->2/3, 0x03->4 vs our 4/5,1/2,3) but that remap is a separate concern, not this finding.

claim: Our reimplementation does case matching inside op_case (pc += skip+4 on no-match), whereas the ORIGINAL does all case matching INSIDE the Switch handler and the standalone Case handler is a bare `pc += 6`. The skip base differs: original adds block_length to the Case-opcode address (a3 still points at the Case op); ours adds skip+4. Observable PC flow coincides only if block_length is authored to include the 6-byte header the same way op_if assumes. Endianness (the audit focus) is correct; the skip-base arithmetic is unverified against multi-case authored data.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:149706-149714 (Switch case-scan: no-match path `addiu a3,a3,0x6` only when reading next, but skip is `addu a3,a3,t0` with a3 = Case-op address) vs standalone Case LAB_8003fb38 ghidra1_V2.txt:149725-149731 (`addiu v0,v0,0x6` = unconditional pc+=6).
fix: To be byte-true to the structure, fold case matching into op_switch (walk Case/Default/EndSwitch entries, set PC to the matching body) and make op_case an unconditional pc+=6; then the skip base (a3 + block_length, a3 = Case-op addr) is exact. Otherwise verify block_length authoring on a multi-case room before trusting `skip+4`. ROOM1170 uses no multi-case switch so it is latent.

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:149711-149714 (Switch no-match: `beq v1,v0` with delay-slot `_addiu a3,a3,0x6` at :149712 runs UNCONDITIONALLY, then `j LAB_8003fadc` with delay-slot `_addu a3,a3,t0` at :149714 -> a3 = Case_addr + 6 + block_length). Loop entry a3 = Switch_addr+4 = first Case addr (ghidra1_V2.txt:149674). Our op_case no-match = Case_addr + block_length + 4 at psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/scd_vm.c:1115 -> off by 2. Data proof room1030/sub00.scd switch@74: Case bl=0x0e at rel4/24/44 -> original(+6+bl)=rel24/44/64 all land on next Case 0x14; ours(+4+bl)=rel22/42/62 all land on 0x1a mid-body.
verdict.fix_summary: A real off-by-2 deviation EXISTS in op_case, but the prior audit's description/cite/fix are WRONG. (1) The audit misread the disasm: it claims original no-match = `a3 + block_length` (a3=Case-op addr), missing that the branch-delay `addiu a3,a3,0x6` at ghidra1_V2.txt:149712 executes unconditionally before `addu a3,a3,t0` (:149714). The true original = Case_addr + 6 + block_length (block_length counts body-after-the-6-byte-Case-header). (2) The audit says ours OVERSHOOTS by 4; it actually UNDERSHOOTS by 2 (our `skip+4` vs correct `skip+6`), landing 2 bytes inside the prior case body. (3) The proposed structural fix (fold matching into op_switch, make op_case a bare pc+=6) is unnecessary and mismodels our interpreter (our op_switch is a no-op that lands on the first Case; op_case does per-case work, mirroring the original's in-loop scan). The correct minimal byte-true fix: change scd_vm.c:1115 from `t->pc += skip + 4;` to `t->pc += skip + 6;` (match path pc+=6 at :1113 is already correct). Single shared file -> fixes PSX + PC. Latent in ROOM1170 (no multi-case switch) but live in room1010/1011/1030 etc. Note: our op_case skip is read as int16 (signed) which is fine; the only error is the +4 vs +6 constant.

claim: Member_cmp 0x3E reads its compare value with scd_read_be_s16(&t->pc[4]) = BIG-ENDIAN. The original reads it `lh` = LITTLE-ENDIAN. This is a wrong-endian operand read (the in-scope kind of bug). The code comment even claims 'Java reads value as BE' but the actual PSX handler uses lh.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:151424 (LAB_80041290, dispatch entry ghidra1_V2.txt:243099 → (0x800745a0-0x800744a8)/4 = 0x3E): `lh s1,0x4(v0)` = signed LE halfword at pc[4..5].
fix: Replace `scd_read_be_s16(&t->pc[4])` with `scd_read_le_s16(&t->pc[4])` in op_member_cmp. member_id=pc[2] and cmp_op=pc[3] are already correct (`lhu s0,0x2(v0); andi a1,s0,0xff` low byte = id, `sra s0,s0,0x8` high byte = op).

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:151424 (LAB_80041290: `lh s1,0x4(v0)` = signed LE halfword pc[4..5]); dispatch entry ghidra1_V2.txt:243099 (0x800745a0 addr LAB_80041290); table base PTR_LAB_800744a8 ghidra1_V2.txt:82707 → (0x800745a0-0x800744a8)/4 = 0x3E. Our code: psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/scd_vm.c:2132 uses scd_read_be_s16.
verdict.fix_summary: In op_member_cmp (scd_vm.c:2132) replace `scd_read_be_s16(&t->pc[4])` with `scd_read_le_s16(&t->pc[4])`. The original handler LAB_80041290 reads the compare value with `lh s1,0x4(v0)` (MIPS R3000 lh is little-endian on PSX), so the value path is LITTLE-ENDIAN, not BE. member_id=pc[2] (andi a1,s0,0xff, low byte) and cmp_op=pc[3] (sra s0,s0,0x8, high byte) are already correct. The in-code comment at lines 2148-2150 claiming a 'genuinely mixed-endian' BE value path is wrong — the disasm uses plain lh. Note: our cmp_op switch maps 0=eq,1=ne,2=lt,3=gt; the disasm caseD_0=xor/sltiu(eq), caseD_1=slt s1,v1(v1>s1 i.e. cur>arg), caseD_2=slt v1,s1 xori 1, etc. — the op-table semantics should also be re-verified separately, but the endianness fix is independent and confirmed.

claim: Member_cmp 0x3E operator switch is wrong: our cases are 0===,1=!=,2=<,3=> (and 4/5/6 missing). The original 7-op table is 0===,1=>,2=>=,3=<,4=<=,5=!=,6=bittest.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:151444-151468 (switchD_800412f0: case0 xor→==; case1 `_slt v0,s1,v1`=val>imm→'>'; case2 `slt v0,v1,s1`+xori→val>=imm; case3 `_slt v0,v1,s1`=val<imm; case4 `slt v0,s1,v1`+xori→val<=imm; case5 xor→!=; case6 and→bittest).
fix: Set op_member_cmp cases to: 0:(cur==arg), 1:(cur>arg), 2:(cur>=arg), 3:(cur<arg), 4:(cur<=arg), 5:(cur!=arg), 6:((cur&arg)!=0), default:0. (Same 7-op table as op_cmp.)

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:151444-151468 (switchD_800412f0: c0 xor+sltiu→==; c1 `slt v0,s1,v1`→v1>s1=>; c2 `slt v0,v1,s1;xori`→v1>=s1; c3 `slt v0,v1,s1`→v1<s1; c4 `slt v0,s1,v1;xori`→v1<=s1; c5 xor+sltu→!=; c6 and+sltu→bittest). Opcode index proof: handler entry LAB_80041290 is referenced by dispatch slot 800745a0 (ghidra1_V2.txt:151416, 243099); table base PTR_LAB_800744a8 (243032) → (0x800745a0-0x800744a8)/4 = 0x3E = Member_cmp. Our code: psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/scd_vm.c:2139-2145 has only 0:==,1:!=,2:<,3:>,default:0.
verdict.fix_summary: Confirmed deviation. Our op_member_cmp (scd_vm.c:2139-2145) implements a wrong 4-entry table (0:==,1:!=,2:<,3:>) and omits cases 4/5/6. The original switchD_800412f0 is a 7-op table identical to Cmp/op_cmp: case0:(cur==arg), case1:(cur>arg), case2:(cur>=arg), case3:(cur<arg), case4:(cur<=arg), case5:(cur!=arg), case6:((cur&arg)!=0), default:0. Cases 1,2,3 currently produce WRONG results (1 should be > not !=; 2 should be >= not <; 3 should be < not >) and 4/5/6 always evaluate false. Fix: replace the switch with the same 7-op table used by op_cmp at scd_vm.c:2110-2119. SEPARATE corroborating bug in the same handler: the operand is read via scd_read_be_s16(&pc[4]), but the disasm uses `lh s1,0x4(v0)` (151424) = native MIPS-LE halfword → operand is LITTLE-endian; the in-code comment defending BE (lines 2148-2151) is incorrect and should be re-examined (out of scope of this claim but should be fixed together).

claim: Cmp 0x23 reads var index from t->pc[1]; the original reads it from the LOW byte of the LE halfword at pc[2] (i.e. var index = pc[2], operator = pc[3]). Our pc[1] is effectively always a different/zero var. The code comment admits this ('engine LAB_8003ff68 reads the var from pc[2]') but kept pc[1] because a prior pc[2] change crashed PSX boot.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:150019-150030 (LAB_8003ff68, dispatch entry ghidra1_V2.txt:243072 → opcode 0x23): `lhu v1,0x2(v0)` then `andi v0,v1,0xff` (var idx = pc[2] low byte) and `srl a2,v1,0x8` (operator = pc[3]); var loaded `lh DAT_800b0fd0[idx*2]` (LE).
fix: Read var index from pc[2] (low byte of the LE halfword) and operator from pc[3]: `uint8_t var_idx = t->pc[2]; uint8_t op = t->pc[3];`. NOTE: this previously crashed PSX boot per the in-code comment — re-introduce ONLY with a verified PSX boot (DuckStation psxtest.sh), since the boot Cmp branches change. Immediate at pc[4..5] LE is already correct.

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:150017-150030 (LAB_8003ff68 Cmp handler): `lw v0,0x1c(a0)` loads the SCD pc; `lhu v1,0x2(v0)` loads the LE u16 at pc[2..3]; `lh a1,0x4(v0)` loads the imm at pc[4..5]; `addiu v0,v0,0x6` (pc+=6); `andi v0,v1,0xff` => var idx = LOW byte = pc[2]; `srl a2,v1,0x8` => operator = HIGH byte = pc[3]; var loaded via `lh a0,0x0(at)=>DAT_800b0fd0` at idx*2 (LE s16). Dispatch proof: table base PTR_LAB_800744a8 = 0x800744a8 (ghidra1_V2.txt:243037); entry 0x80074534 -> LAB_8003ff68 (ghidra1_V2.txt:243072); index = (0x80074534-0x800744a8)/4 = 0x23 = Cmp. Contrast Switch(0x13) LAB_8003fa5c (ghidra1_V2.txt:149673) `lbu a2,0x1(a3)` => Switch genuinely reads var from pc[1], proving per-opcode layouts differ and that Cmp's pc[2] read is not a misread. Our code psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/scd_vm.c:2105 reads `var_idx = t->pc[1]` => provable deviation.
verdict.fix_summary: In op_cmp (scd_vm.c:2105) change `var_idx = t->pc[1]` to `var_idx = t->pc[2]` (low byte of the LE halfword at pc[2]); op stays pc[3] and imm stays LE s16 at pc[4..5] (both already correct). The current code effectively always compares work_vars[pc[1]] (a reserved/padding byte, usually 0) instead of the real var. CAVEAT from our own code comment: a prior pc[2] trial crashed PSX boot (altered ROOM1170 boot Cmp branches -> psxspu timeout, PC=0), while the PC/SDL port tolerated it. So this is a real deviation that must be fixed, but the fix must be re-introduced ONLY with a verified PSX boot via DuckStation psxtest.sh (>=40s graceful close), and the boot-crash root cause investigated (it indicates a real Cmp branch in ROOM1170 boot that our other state was masking with the wrong var). The PC build can take the fix immediately.

claim: Cmp 0x23 operator cases 2/3/4 are swapped vs the original. Ours: 2=<, 3=<=, 4=>=. Original: 2=>=, 3=<, 4=<=. (cases 0===, 1=>, 5=!=, 6=bittest are correct.)
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:150042-150066 (switchD_8003ffc0, where a0=var, a1=imm: case0 xor→==; case1 `_slt v0,a1,a0`=var>imm; case2 `slt v0,a0,a1`+`_xori 1`=!(var<imm)=var>=imm; case3 `_slt v0,a0,a1`=var<imm; case4 `slt v0,a1,a0`+`_xori 1`=!(var>imm)=var<=imm; case5 xor→!=; case6 and→bittest).
fix: Reorder op_cmp cases to match: 0:(v==imm),1:(v>imm),2:(v>=imm),3:(v<imm),4:(v<=imm),5:(v!=imm),6:((v&imm)!=0),default:0.

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:150049-150058 (switchD_8003ffc0 case2/3/4). With a0=var (loaded lh a0,DAT_800b0fd0+var<<1 @150030) and a1=imm (lh a1,0x4(v0) @150020): case2 (8003ffdc) `slt v0,a0,a1`+`_xori 1`=!(var<imm)=var>=imm; case3 (8003ffe8) `_slt v0,a0,a1`=var<imm; case4 (8003fff0) `slt v0,a1,a0`+`_xori 1`=!(imm<var)=var<=imm. Our scd_vm.c:2113-2115 has case2=(v<imm), case3=(v<=imm), case4=(v>=imm) — cases 2/3/4 are swapped. cases 0/1/5/6 verified correct (==,>,!=,bittest).
verdict.fix_summary: In op_cmp (psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/scd_vm.c:2111-2118) reorder operator cases to match the disasm switch: 0:(v==imm), 1:(v>imm), 2:(v>=imm), 3:(v<imm), 4:(v<=imm), 5:(v!=imm), 6:((v&imm)!=0), default:0. I.e. case2 < => >=, case3 <= => <, case4 >= => <=. Note: this op_cmp also reads the var from pc[1] while the engine LAB_8003ff68 reads pc[2] (separate, already-documented latent issue) — that is independent of the operator-table swap and not part of this fix.

claim: The GRAB struggle 'mash the face buttons to build an escape counter' mechanic is fabricated. The comment claims it is byte-true ('FUN_80011f50 raises the kick-off flag when the struggle counter reaches the type-7 hit'), but FUN_80011f50 is an ENEMY hit-detection routine — its param_1==7/8 path sets flag 0x40 on the HIT ENTITY (puVar12+0x93), it is NOT a player grab-struggle/escape counter and reads no pad. The real player GRAB state (major-state 5) handler dispatches a fixed sub-state animation sequence with NO pad read and NO escape counter.
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:132798 (LAB_80036834 = player state-5/GRAB handler: on entry sets displacement from DAT_800acad6, then jalr DAT_800ac758[entity[8]<<2] — a sub-state anim handler, no pad/no counter) + RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80011f50.c:146-147 (param_1==7/8 sets entity[0x93]|=0x40, an enemy-hit flag, not a player counter)
fix: Remove the button-mash struggle wiring (re15_player_grab_struggle call in game_step + s_grab_struggle/PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_MAX/DECAY/kick-off path in player_damage_common.c). Drive player state 5 (grab) and state 6 (eaten) as fixed sub-state anim sequences via the entity[8]-indexed sub-handler model (DAT_800ac758/DAT_800ac858), matching the 6 reference savestates where Leon is eaten (hp 100→-1) with no escape. The kick-off (state→recover) only exists if a sub-handler in DAT_800ac758 implements it — RE that overlay/runtime table before re-adding any escape.

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80011f50.c:145-147 (param_1==7/8 → entity[0x93]|=0x40 on the HIT entity; iterates DAT_800acc2c stride 0x7d, dispatches PTR_LAB_8006e548[param_1] hit-test, entity TYPE = puVar12[2] checked <0x20 at line 170; no pad, no counter = an enemy-hit resolver) + ghidra1_V2.txt:132798-132830 (LAB_80036834 = player major-state handler, dispatch-table 0x80073f98 entry @0x80073fa4 per ghidra1_V2.txt:242025: on entry sets displacement from DAT_800acad6, loads entity DAT_800acbfc, reads entity[8]<<2 → DAT_800ac758[idx] → jalr a fixed sub-state anim handler; NO pad read, NO escape counter) + ghidra1_V2.txt:132687-132704 (sibling state handler LAB_800366bc shares the identical pad-less sub-state dispatch shape). Our fabricated code: psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/player_damage_common.c:50-51,65,80-84,163,170-179 (s_grab_struggle mash counter / PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_MAX/DECAY / >=MAX kick-off) wired from game_step_common.c:47-51.
verdict.fix_summary: Remove the fabricated button-mash escape mechanic: delete the re15_player_grab_struggle() call (game_step_common.c:47-51) and the s_grab_struggle / PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_MAX / PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_DECAY / '>=MAX → kick-off' branch in player_damage_common.c (lines ~50-51,65,80-84,163,170-179), and correct the false 'byte-true FUN_80011f50' comment (that func is an enemy hit-resolver: param_1==7/8 sets enemy entity[0x93]|=0x40, not a player counter; it reads no pad). Drive player major-state GRAB (and EATEN) as a fixed entity[8]-indexed sub-state animation sequence via the DAT_800ac758 sub-handler model (LAB_80036834), matching the 6 reference savestates where Leon is eaten hp100→-1 with no escape. Only re-add a kick-off/recover transition if/when a DAT_800ac758 sub-handler is RE'd to implement one — do not synthesize it.

claim: PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_MAX=100 / PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_DECAY=1 / PLY_KICK_FRAMES=14 / PLY_KICK_STEP=45 and the entire 'broke free → KICK-OFF, step back' phase-2 are tuned magic numbers with no disasm basis. The original grab state (LAB_80036834) has no struggle threshold; the eaten state (LAB_800368c0) runs a fixed sub-state sequence to game-over (state 7).
disasm_cite: ghidra1_V2.txt:132835 (LAB_800368c0 = player state-6/EATEN handler: jalr DAT_800ac858[entity[8]<<2], a fixed sub-state sequence; no HP-based escape, no pad) + ghidra1_V2.txt:242025-242027 (PTR_LAB_80073f90 state5=0x80036834,state6=0x800368c0,state7=0x8003694c game-over)
fix: Drop the kick-off escape phase and its 4 tunables; the eaten path should end in PLY_GAMEOVER (state 7) as the byte-true sequence does. If a kick-off truly exists it must come from RE'ing the DAT_800ac758 sub-handler table (runtime-filled — trace via DuckStation), not invented constants.

verdict.proof: ghidra1_V2.txt:132835-132871 (LAB_800368c0 state-6/EATEN handler: reads sub-state entity[8] via DAT_800acbfc+8, indexes runtime table DAT_800ac858, jalr — a FIXED sub-state dispatch with NO HP compare, NO pad read, NO struggle/kick); ghidra1_V2.txt:132798-132834 (LAB_80036834 state-5/GRAB handler — structurally identical, same DAT_800ac758[entity[8]<<2] dispatch, also no struggle threshold / kick step); ghidra1_V2.txt:242025-242027 (PTR_LAB_80073f90 state5=80036834, state6=800368c0, state7=8003694c game-over); ghidra1_V2.txt:77698-77714 (damage entry FUN_80012d60 only ever writes state byte DAT_800aca58 = 2 HIT or 3 DEATH per HP bgez, never a struggle/kick escape); ghidra1_V2.txt:132786 (LAB_80036804 sets state=0x7 game-over after FUN_80045630). vs our psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/player_damage_common.c:50-53,170-190 (PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_MAX=100/DECAY=1/PLY_KICK_FRAMES=14/PLY_KICK_STEP=45 + the entire phase-2 "broke free -> KICK-OFF, step back" branch).
verdict.fix_summary: Real, citable deviation — could not refute. The state-5 GRAB (LAB_80036834) and state-6 EATEN (LAB_800368c0) handlers are byte-identical fixed sub-state jump-table dispatchers with NO HP-based escape, NO pad/mash read, and NO kick step; the only documented exit is the eaten sub-sequence advancing to state 7 game-over (LAB_8003694c). Our PLY_GRAB_STRUGGLE_MAX(100)/DECAY(1)/PLY_KICK_FRAMES(14)/PLY_KICK_STEP(45) and the whole phase-2 'broke free -> KICK-OFF, step back' branch (lines 170-190) are invented constants with no disasm basis (the comments even self-flag 'mash counter','tuned'). Byte-true fix: delete the 4 tunables and phase-2; once HP<0 in GRAB, transition GRAB(5)->EATEN(6) and run a fixed sub-state advance to PLY_GAMEOVER(7) with no escape. If a real kick-off exists it must be RE'd from the runtime-filled DAT_800ac758/DAT_800ac858 sub-handler tables via a DuckStation RAM trace, not synthesized. NOTE: also independently suspect at lines 33-38 — PLY_HIT_FRAMES/DEATH_FRAMES/KB_FRAMES/KB_STEP are flagged 'tunable' (original ends on the clip's own keyframe wrap), a separate latent deviation not part of this finding.

claim: PLY_GRAB_BITE_PERIOD=40 / PLY_GRAB_BITE_DMG=10 'periodic bite while held' is fabricated. In the original the bite damage is applied once via the damage-table path at grab time (FUN_80012d60 subtracts DAT_8006f418[type]); the grab/eaten states are animation-timer driven, not a per-40-frame HP drip.
disasm_cite: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80012d60.c:38 (DAT_800acaee -= DAT_8006f418[type] — single damage application) + ghidra1_V2.txt:132835 (state-6 EATEN handler is anim sub-state only, no periodic HP subtraction)
fix: Remove the s_grab_bite_t periodic-damage loop. Apply the grab damage once through the existing damage-table path; let the eaten state run as a fixed anim sequence to game-over.

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80012d60.c:37-38,51-56 + ghidra1_V2.txt:77659-77675,77705-77713 (the real damage path) and ghidra1_V2.txt:132835 / 242026 (LAB_800368c0 = state-6 EATEN dispatcher, pure anim sub-state, no HP write) and the USER ground-truth savestates (zombie_attack_anim_mechanism_savestates_2026_06_20.md:10 — Leon HP 100->90->65->40->-1 across the grab).
verdict.fix_summary: Our PLY_GRAB_BITE_PERIOD=40 / PLY_GRAB_BITE_DMG=10 periodic-drip (player_damage_common.c:48-49,164-167) IS a fabricated magic-number mechanism with no disasm basis -> it is a real deviation, so the audit is RIGHT that the 40-frame drip must go. BUT the audit's PROPOSED FIX ('apply once, then fixed anim to game-over') is itself NOT byte-true and must NOT be applied: the USER's own 6 reference savestates record HP dropping in FOUR steps (100->90->65->40->-1), i.e. multiple damage applications during the held grab, not one. The byte-true mechanism (proved in FUN_80012d60 @ ghidra1_V2.txt:77659-77713): each accepted hit subtracts ONE table value DAT_8006f418[type] from HP (DAT_800acaee, entity+0x9a), gated by the i-frame latch DAT_800acae7&1 (line 77661) which is SET on hit (line 77710) and CLEARED when the hit/stagger sub-state ends; if HP<0 it sets state 3 (DEATH). So damage recurs once per attack-collision cycle as the latch clears (NOT every 40 frames, NOT a flat 10, NOT once-total). The EATEN state-6 handler LAB_800368c0 itself writes NO HP (confirmed) — death is reached purely by the latch-gated re-hits driving HP<0. CORRECT FIX: drive the grab/eaten HP loss through the existing re15_player_damage() table path re-invoked by the zombie's attack collision under the i-frame latch (clear the latch at stagger-end), removing the invented 40/10 constants; let state-6 run anim-only with the death transition owned by HP<0.

claim: re15_to_re2_member_id case 8 returns RE2 id 0x08 with comment 'motion (same ID in both)'. But the RE1.5 Member_set handler writes a STATUS BYTE (8-bit) at entity offset 0x04 for member id 8 — NOT motion. Our chain (translate 8->0x08, then actor_set_member case 0x08 -> re15_actor_set_motion) makes Member_set(8,N) drive the player's animation/motion, whereas the original sets an 8-bit state/status byte at +0x04. The 'same ID in both' justification is false for RE1.5.
disasm_cite: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8004116c.c:34-35 (case 8: *(undefined1*)(param_1+1)=uVar1, i.e. byte write to entity+0x04)
fix: Map RE1.5 member 8 to the RE2 8-bit field at +0x04 (state) instead of motion. In re15_to_re2_member_id, case 8 should NOT return 0x08(motion). RE1.5 ids 8/9/10/0xb (status bytes off 0x04/0x05/0x06/0x07) -> RE2 0x02/0x05/0x06/... 8-bit fields; ids 6/7 (off 0x00 type / 0x0C subtype, 32-bit) -> RE2 +0x00/+0x0C. ROOM1170 does not exercise these (only ids 0,1,2,4,18 used), so it is latent, but the table and the 'same in both' comment are wrong.

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8004116c.c:34-35 (case 8: *(undefined1 *)(param_1 + 1) = uVar1 — param_1 is undefined4*, so +1 = entity +0x04, written as the LOW BYTE of param_3, i.e. an 8-bit state field). FUN_8004116c IS the Member_set field dispatcher: ghidra1_V2.txt:151258-151266 shows the Member_set(0x34) handler LAB_800410b8 (dispatch-tbl xref @80074578) loading member_id=lbu pc[1], value=lh pc[2], then jal FUN_8004116c. By contrast RE1.5 'motion'/anim-action lives at entity +0x94, NOT +0x04: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8001f314.c:8 reads *(byte*)(DAT_800ac784 + 0x94) as the clip index. Our chain (re15_to_re2.c:54 case 8→0x08, then actor_common.c:138 case 0x08→re15_actor_set_motion) therefore drives the player's animation instead of writing the +0x04 state byte.
verdict.fix_summary: Real but LATENT deviation (ROOM1170 uses Member_set ids {0,1,2,4,18} only — verified by grep over extracted/PSX/STAGE1/room1170/scd, never id 8 — so nothing visibly breaks today, but the table + 'same ID in both' comment are wrong and any script using Member_set(8,N) would mis-drive Leon's motion). FUN_8004116c case 8 writes an 8-bit field at entity+0x04, which RE2 id 0x02 maps to (actor_common.c:133 case 0x02 -> a->state, RE2 +0x04). Fix: in re15_to_re2_member_id, case 8 should return 0x02 (state), not 0x08 (motion), and fix the comment. For full table consistency the byte fields 8/9/10/0xb (+0x04/+0x05/+0x06/+0x07) -> RE2 0x02/0x03/0x04/0x05; and FUN_8004116c case 6 (+0x00 word)/case 7 (+0x0C word) -> RE2 0x00/0x07 — but the single cited deviation is case 8.

claim: case 6 returns 0x06 and case 7 returns 0x07. In RE1.5, member 6 writes the 32-bit ENTITY TYPE at offset 0x00 and member 7 writes the 32-bit ENTITY SUBTYPE at offset 0x0C. Through our engine, RE2 id 0x06 -> a->sub_state_4 and RE2 id 0x07 is unmapped (silently ignored). So Member_set(6,..) writes a wrong sub-state byte and Member_set(7,..) is dropped entirely vs the original 32-bit type/subtype writes.
disasm_cite: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8004116c.c:28-33 (case 6: *param_1=param_3 [off 0x00, type]; case 7: param_1[3]=param_3 [off 0x0C, subtype])
fix: Translate RE1.5 member 6 -> RE2 entity-type field (+0x00) and member 7 -> RE2 subtype field (+0x0C), and add the corresponding cases in re15_actor_set_member. Latent for ROOM1170 (ids 6/7 unused there).

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8004116c.c:28-33 — case 6: `*param_1 = param_3;` (param_1 is undefined4*, so a 32-bit write at offset 0x00 = entity TYPE); case 7: `param_1[3] = param_3;` (32-bit write at offset 0x0C = entity SUBTYPE). Corroborated by the annotated RE_15_modified_V2/entity_set_field.c:66-74 ("case 6: Entity-Typ (32-bit) - Offset 0x00", "case 7: Entity-SubTyp (32-bit) - Offset 0x0C"). Our deviation: psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/re15_to_re2.c:52-53 maps 6->0x06 / 7->0x07; psx_dev/re15_reborn/src/actor_common.c:137 makes RE2 0x06 = `(uint8_t)value` into sub_state_4 (byte-truncated, wrong field), and 0x07 has NO case so it hits the `default: silently ignore` at actor_common.c:158. Actually executed in a shipped room: extracted/PSX/STAGE1/room11B0/scd/sub06.c:159 `Member_set(6, 513)` and :213 `Member_set(6, 129)` (ROOM11B0.RDT exists in info/Re1.5/PSX/STAGE1). So original writes 0x201/0x81 as a 32-bit type; ours writes (uint8_t)513=0x01 / (uint8_t)129=0x81 into sub_state_4 instead.
verdict.fix_summary: Real deviation, but the proposed fix's target ids are imprecise. In OUR engine RE2 id 0x00 = flags and 0x0C = y (actor_common.c:86,140), neither is type/subtype — so routing member 6->RE2 0x00 and 7->RE2 0x0C would corrupt flags/Y. Correct byte-true fix: route RE1.5 member 6 -> the actor `type` field (re15_actor.h:33) as a FULL 32-bit write (no byte truncation), and add a 32-bit subtype field for member 7 (currently the struct has none). Add explicit cases for these in re15_actor_set_member rather than reusing sub_state_4/dropping. Confirmed latent for ROOM1170 (it never calls Member_set 6/7); the live caller is room11B0/sub06. Not an engine-specific arch difference — it is a translation-table correctness bug.

claim: default branch passes RE1.5 ids 0x0B..0x25 straight through as RE2 ids. But the RE1.5 handler only defines ids up to 0x13, and RE1.5 ids 0x0B..0x13 are 8/16-bit status/flag/model/damage fields at small offsets (0x07,0x09,0x08,0x0A,0x0B,0x1C4,0x98,0x82,0x1BA) — NOT the RE2 coordinate/velocity aliases the passthrough implies. E.g. RE1.5 id 0x11 = model-index@0x98, but passthrough -> RE2 0x11 = a->floor; RE1.5 id 0x12 = damage-flag@0x82, passthrough -> RE2 0x12 (unmapped, dropped).
disasm_cite: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8004116c.c:43-68 (cases 0xb..0x13 write small status/flag/model/damage fields, not coordinates)
fix: Remove the blanket 0x0B..0x25 passthrough; map each RE1.5 id 0x0B..0x13 explicitly to its RE2-engine field per FUN_8004116c. Latent except id 0x12 (see next finding).

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8004116c.c:43-68 (write) + ghidra1_V2.txt:151538-151575 (read FUN_80041358, exact mirror) prove RE1.5 Member ids 0x0B..0x13 = small u8/u16 status fields: 0x0B→byte0x07, 0x0C→0x09, 0x0D→0x08, 0x0E→0x0a, 0x0F→0x0b(u8), 0x10→u16@0x1c4, 0x11→u16@0x98, 0x12→u8@0x82, 0x13→u16@0x1ba. Opcode confirmed Member_set 0x34: ghidra1_V2.txt:243089 (table 0x80074578=LAB_800410b8→FUN_8004116c) with base PTR_LAB_800744a8 at ghidra1_V2.txt:82707. RE2 side FUN_80055cb0 (RE2_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80055cb0.c:45-71) has ids 0x0B/0x0C/0x0D = 4-byte X/Y/Z coords @0x38/0x3c/0x40 and 0x0E..0x10 = rot u16 — completely different fields. Our re15_to_re2.c:59 passes 0x0B..0x25 straight through, so actor_common.c:139-157 routes RE1.5 status-byte writes to RE2 x/y/z/rot. NOT latent: real scripts use these ids — extracted/PSX/STAGE1/room1020/scd/sub03.c:7 Member_set(12,138), room1081/scd/sub01.c:26 Member_cmp(15=1024), room1030/scd/sub09.c:14 Member_set2(16,4).
verdict.fix_summary: Remove the blanket `if (re15_id >= 0x0B && re15_id <= 0x25) return re15_id;` passthrough at re15_to_re2.c:59. RE1.5 only defines ids 0..0x13 (FUN_8004116c: sltiu a1,0x14 → 0x14+ is no-op), and ids 0x0B..0x13 are NOT coordinate aliases — they are u8/u16 status/flag/model/damage fields. Map each RE1.5 id explicitly to its RE2-engine equivalent field per FUN_8004116c/FUN_80055cb0, OR (cleaner) bypass the RE2 translation for these and write the RE1.5 struct fields directly. Concretely the RE1.5 reads/writes are: 0x0B=u8@0x07, 0x0C=u8@0x09, 0x0D=u8@0x08, 0x0E=u8@0x0a, 0x0F=u8@0x0b, 0x10=u16@0x1c4, 0x11=u16@0x98 (model-index), 0x12=u8@0x82 (damage-flag, currently dropped: no RE2 case 0x12 in actor_common.c), 0x13=u16@0x1ba. ids 0x14..0x25 must return -1 (RE1.5 drops them) — not pass through. ROOM1170 is unaffected (uses only ids 0/1/2/4); the bug bites multi-room rooms 1020/1030/1041/1080/1081 that already ship these ids.

claim: ROOM1170 actually executes Member_set(18, 4) (id 0x12). Through re15_to_re2_member_id it returns 0x12 (passthrough), and re15_actor_set_member has no case 0x12, so the write is SILENTLY DROPPED. The RE1.5 original writes byte value 4 to entity offset 0x82 (a real field read in 15 decompiled functions — a hit/damage flag). So a cinematic field set is lost vs the original.
disasm_cite: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8004116c.c:64-65 (case 0x12: *(undefined1*)((int)param_1+0x82)=uVar1)
fix: Add a member-0x12 mapping that writes the 8-bit field at the actor's equivalent of +0x82 (add the field to re15_actor_t and a case 0x12 in re15_actor_set_member). This is the only id 6-0x13 case that ROOM1170 exercises, so it is the one to fix first.

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_8004116c.c:64-65 (case 0x12: *(undefined1*)((int)param_1+0x82)=uVar1); dispatch verified in ghidra1_V2.txt:151257-151267 — Member_set opcode handler LAB_800410b8 (XREF 0x80074578) loads `lbu a1,0x1(v0)` = RAW script member id (no translation) and `lh a2,0x2(v0)` = value, then `jal FUN_8004116c`, so Member_set(18,4) → FUN_8004116c(entity,0x12,4) → entity+0x82=4. ROOM1170 executes it at extracted/PSX/STAGE1/room1170/scd/sub14.c:11 (Member_set(18,4)). Field +0x82 is a live read affecting control flow, e.g. RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80031c44.c:15 (`*(byte*)(iVar1+0x82)+1 == DAT_800acad6` gates flag 0x4000). Our path drops it: scd_vm.c:2040 re15_to_re2_member_id(18) hits default branch (re15_to_re2.c:58-60, 0x12 in [0x0B,0x25] → returns 0x12 passthrough), scd_vm.c:2042 calls re15_actor_set_member(ws,0x12,4), and actor_common.c:131-159 has NO case 0x12 → line 158 default silently ignores. Write lost vs original.
verdict.fix_summary: Add an 8-bit field mirroring entity+0x82 (per FUN_8004116c case 0x12 it is a 1-byte field; the 14 reader funcs treat it as a hit/state flag) to re15_actor_t, and add `case 0x12: a-><field> = (uint8_t)value; break;` to re15_actor_set_member in actor_common.c. Since RE1.5 id 0x12 passes through re15_to_re2_member_id unchanged, key the new case directly on 0x12 — no translation-table change needed. This is the only member id in the 6..0x13 range ROOM1170 exercises, so it is the one to land.

claim: Integer isqrt replaces libgte SquareRoot0/VectorNormal. This is an engine_specific perf substitution (R3000 has no FPU; PC uses sqrt()). SquareRoot0 returns floor(sqrt) which the bit-by-bit isqrt also returns, so the dist/range integer math matches the original's integer SquareRoot0 result.
disasm_cite: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80053fc0.c:39 (SquareRoot0(dx*dx+dz*dz)) and FUN_800542dc.c:7 (VectorNormal)
fix: none (integer isqrt == SquareRoot0 floor result; legit FPU-less substitution accepted per project notes)

verdict.proof: RE_15_Quellcode_V2/FUN_80053fc0.c:39 (dist = SquareRoot0(dx*dx+dz*dz)) and FUN_800542dc.c:7 (VectorNormal) are real and correctly cited. The REFUTATION is of the audit's equivalence claim, proven against the actual libgte source info/Resident_Evil_und_Playstation_Information/PSn00bSDK-60deeff/PSn00bSDK-master/libpsn00b/psxgte/squareroot.s:58-94 (SquareRoot0) and vectornormals.s:11-79 (VectorNormalS). SquareRoot0 is NOT floor(sqrt): it is a LZCR-normalize + 192-entry sqrt_table[] (squareroot.s:99) 12-bit-mantissa APPROXIMATION (srl v0,12 at squareroot.s:91). Faithful simulation of that asm shows SquareRoot0(289)=16 while floor(sqrt(289))=17, and the same floor-minus-1 at every perfect square >=289 (361->18 vs 19, 529->22 vs 23, ...). Our re15_isqrt (light_common.c:18-30) returns EXACT floor (0 mismatches vs math.isqrt across 320k samples), so SquareRoot0 != re15_isqrt on ~95% of inputs (minimum off-by-one near squares, larger via mantissa rounding). VectorNormal (vectornormals.s) is likewise a _norm_table[] reciprocal-sqrt approximation (vectornormals.s:56,66 nGPF), not the exact (x<<12)/isqrt in light_common.c:129-131. dist feeds atten=range-dist; color=byte*atten/range (FUN_80053fc0.c:45,53,80) -> a +1 dist error changes 8-bit RGB output, so the result is not byte-identical.
verdict.fix_summary: Replace re15_isqrt with a byte-exact port of libgte SquareRoot0 (LZCR leading-zero normalize -> 192-entry sqrt_table[] from squareroot.s:99-123, with the 0xa1d5 typo corrected to 0x1ad5, then >>12), and replace vec3_normalize_q12 with a byte-exact port of VectorNormalS (_norm_table[] reciprocal-sqrt from vectornormals.s:85-108 + the nSQR/nGPF mantissa-shift fold). A generic bit-by-bit/double isqrt yields different integer results than the GTE table approximation (e.g. 289->16 not 17), so the lighting falloff (dist) and light directions are off by small amounts vs the original. The intent (no R3000 soft-float) is correct and accepted; only the chosen integer approximation must match libgte's table result exactly to be byte-true.
